paper_title: Stress-Testing the Reasoning Competence of LLMs With Proofs Under Minimal Formalism

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: We introduce ProofGrid, a benchmark suite for evaluating LLM reasoning through machine-checkable proofs rather than final answers alone. ProofGrid contains 15 tasks spanning proof writing, proof checking, proof masking, and proof gap-filling. Tasks are expressed in minimal formal notation, especially NDL, a compact natural-deduction language that fits in short prompts and supports precise, auditable verification. This yields mechanical, reproducible, and fine-grained evaluation rather than judgments by humans or LLMs. ProofGrid covers a calibrated difficulty spectrum, from foundational reasoning tests to structurally rich challenge tasks that no current model solves, while minimizing reliance on domain knowledge, solver delegation, and long-context artifacts. We also develop a comparative framework for reasoning benchmarks and use it to situate ProofGrid relative to existing work in terms of representation, verification guarantees, and reasoning depth. Methodologically, we introduce an instrumented proof-checking pipeline that tolerates minor surface deviations while locating the first substantive reasoning failure, improving measurement resolution and separating proof planning from low-level execution noise. Using this pipeline, we evaluate a broad range of open and proprietary models. Results show rapid progress but substantial remaining limits: frontier models perform well on several foundational tasks, yet difficult tasks, especially those requiring global combinatorial reasoning or low-level proof synthesis, remain far from solved. We also identify epistemic instability, where models generate flawed proofs yet correctly reject those local inferences in isolation, and formalize this with an Epistemic Stability Index. Finally, we complement accuracy with 2PL IRT analyses, Wright maps, and a normalized task-discrimination measure based on Fisher information.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: Stress-Testing the Reasoning Competence of LLMs With Proofs Under Minimal Formalism (Extended Technical Report) 6 2 0 2 7 ] . [ 1 4 2 5 2 1 . 5 0 6 2 : r Konstantine Arkoudas konstantine.arkoudas@gmail.com Serafim Batzoglou serafim.batzoglou@gmail.com

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: We introduce PROOFGRID, challenging benchmark suite for evaluating the reasoning competence of language models through machine-checkable proofs rather than final answers alone. PROOFGRID spans 15 tasks organized around proof writing, proof checking, proof masking, and proof gap-filling. The tasks are expressed in deliberately minimal formal notation, most notably NDL, stripped-down language for natural deduction that fits in short prompt and supports precise, auditable checking. As result, correctness judgments are mechanical, reproducible, and fine-grained rather than dependent on human or LLM judges. PROOFGRID discriminates model capabilities with high resolution and covers carefully calibrated difficulty spectrum, from foundational reasoning tests to structurally rich challenge tasks that no model currently solves, while avoiding reliance on domain knowledge, problems that can be easily outsourced to solvers, or long-context artifacts. The paper develops detailed comparative framework for reasoning benchmarks and uses it to survey and systematize broad and previously fragmented literature. This analysis clarifies how PROOFGRID differs from existing benchmarks not only in difficulty, but in representational choices, verification guarantees, reasoning depth, and vulnerability to shortcut-based success. key methodological contribution of the paper is an instrumented proof-checking pipeline that tolerates minor surface-level deviations while explicitly identifying the first substantive failure in stretch of reasoning. This significantly sharpens measurement resolution, separating high-level proof planning from low-level execution noise, and reveals that current frontier models often possess surprisingly strong proof-strategy capabilities even when their formal proof execution is brittle. Using this pipeline, we conduct an extensive evaluation and analysis of broad set of open and proprietary models released from late 2024 until now. Our results demonstrate strikingly rapid advances as well as sharp remaining limitations. Frontier systems now perform strongly on several foundational tasks, yet difficult PROOFGRID tasks (especially those requiring global combinatorial reasoning or low-level proof synthesis) remain far from solved. Beyond task-level accuracy, we identify widespread epistemic instability, e.g., models often generate proofs containing local logical errors that they can nevertheless recognize and reject when those same inferences are presented in isolation. We formalize this phenomenon via an Epistemic Stability Index (ESI), quantitative measure of cross-context coherence between model outputs for different but related tasks (such as generated proofs and corresponding entailment judgments). Finally, we complement raw accuracies with 2PL Item Response Theory (IRT) analyses, Wright maps, and new normalized measure of task discrimination based on Fisher information and latent model abilities estimated from their responses. Taken together, these results position PROOFGRID not merely as reasoning benchmark, but as diagnostic framework for studying the emerging structure, limits, and internal coherence of reasoning in contemporary language models. This article is an extended version of the 2025 EMNLP Findings paper Stress-Testing the Reasoning Competence of LLMs With Formal Proofs. It contains substantial new material beyond the conference version, including new tasks, results, methods, and analyses. Corresponding author

sections.title: 1 Introduction
sections.content: Large language models (LLMs) have advanced rapidly, finding applications in increasingly complex problems well beyond their original scope of narrow NLP tasks like summarization, information extraction, and composition of short texts like emails. The recent emergence of the inference-compute paradigm [Snell et al. 2025] has given rise to so-called large reasoning models (LRMs), such as OpenAIs o-series of models along with GPT-5 and its successors; DeepSeeks R1 and related offerings; Anthropics Claude Sonnet and Opus lines; and Googles Gemini family. These models have been trained with reinforcement learning (RL) and can perform test-time search to identify promising completions that are more likely to yield correct answers, at the expense of higher inference costs. Powered by test-time scaling and RL, language models have demonstrated dramatic improvements in logical and mathematical reasoning, with some of them surpassing human performance on highly challenging benchmarks, such as FrontierMath [Glazer et al. 2024]. However, most existing test batteries for logical reasoning are focused only on the accuracy of the answers, which are typically either binary (in response to yes/no questions); or answer keys to multiple-choice questions; or, increasingly, numbers or other boxed{ } literals, such as closed-form expressions. While this approach enables automatic evaluation by simply comparing model answers against canonical solutions, it has serious drawback: it ignores the reasoning that gives rise to the answers. This can inflate performance, because models sometimes arrive at the right answers for the wrong reasons, through plausible but ultimately spurious inferences. More fundamentally, this evaluation mode is particularly unsuitable for mathematics, where proofs are not just means to an end but the very foundation of the discipline. mathematical claim is accepted not because it seems likely or even because it happens to be correct in some instances, but because rigorous and logically sound proof demonstrating its truth is given. Therefore, emphasizing answer accuracy while overlooking the validity of the underlying reasoning is fundamentally misaligned with mathematical practice, which typically rejects result that is derived by faulty reasoning, even if it is numerically correct. To properly assess the reasoning abilities of language models, especially in mathematics, it is essential to evaluate whether they can construct and verify proofs. PROOFGRID is step in that direction. PROOFGRID is benchmark suite for evaluating proof-based reasoning, requiring models not merely to produce correct answers but to construct, verify, and repair proofs expressed in minimalist natural-deduction language (NDL) for propositional logic, and also in pared-down formulation of pure equational logic. The benchmark comprises multiple tasks (proof writing, proof checking, proof masking, and proof gap-filling), designed to probe complementary aspects of reasoning, from generation of complex inference chains to error localization and structural reconstruction. In this paper we present and discuss all tasks in detail, evaluate 24 well-known models (both closedand open-source) on every task, present and discuss the results, and perform extensive error analysis. All tasks in PROOFGRID are formulated in uninterpreted formal logic. As result, the problems are entirely abstract and thus require what psychologists call fluid intelligence, as opposed to crystallized intelligence [Sternberg, Robert 2020]. Fluid intelligence refers to innate reasoning ability employed to solve novel problems, while crystallized intelligence reflects the declarative knowledge and problem-solving know-how that has accumulated through explicit instruction. Most current benchmarks allow models to leverage their vast stores of crystallized knowledge, acquired through their pretraining, which often enables them to essentially retrieve correct answers, either through brute memorization or by employing shortcuts that are sometimes valid but are other times derived from superficial patterns. There is nothing wrong with shortcuts, of course, especially when they are based on sound theory. On the contrary, they are invaluable in practice. But when it comes to assessing native reasoning ability, they can act as confounders. If we ask model whether graph can be colored with 2 colors, it can answer immediately if it notices an odd-length cycle and it happens to know that graph is 2-colorable iff it contains no odd-length cycles. By minimizing reliance on memorized content, symbolic-logic problems are much better gauges of pure reasoning competence. To make the ramifications of this concrete with an example: o4-mini was touted as achieving 99.5% pass@1 on the 2025 AIME exam, slightly beating o3. Even more impressively, o4-mini-medium scored 22% on the competition subset of FrontierMath, outperforming the average team of human mathematicians ( 19%). On PROOFGRID, by contrast, it consistently places in the bottom half of the 24 models we have evaluated, frequently scoring less than half of the absolute score of o3,1 and well below open-source models like DeepSeek R1 or even DeepSeek 3.1. This performance profile is more consistent with the experiences of users who report that impressive benchmark numbers often fail to translate to strong real-world performance on reasoning-intensive tasks, especially those that gain little from memorized 1For instance, in the PL3-PW division of PROOFGRID, the accuracy of o4-mini is 6% compared to o3s 22%. 2 knowledge. An advantage of abstract reasoning problems like those of PROOFGRID is that they can tease apart stronger from weaker models much more reliably. To quantify this with precision, we introduce simple notion of spread we call DI (discriminability index), which we compute and report for every task in PROOFGRID. We also draw on IRT and statistical information theory to introduce new normalized metric of task discrimination on select bands of latent model ability. In addition, many existing benchmarks have become ceiling-saturated and no longer pose serious challenge. PROOFGRID features demanding tasks that reveal the limitations of even the most capable models. We hope this will help to drive continued advances in the intersection of reasoning and language models. Beyond the issue of ceiling effects, with leading scores essentially at 100%, many existing benchmarks cluster together even midand third-tier models. On MATH 500, for example, GPT-4.1 scores 87.6%, while Gemini 2.5 Pro and DeepSeek 3.1 receive 91% and 91.5%, respectively. This amounts to spread of 3.9 points between the strongest and weakest of these three models, and spread of only 0.5% between the top two of the three. By comparison, the corresponding scores from randomly selected PROOFGRID task, PL1-PW, are 35% for GPT-4.1, 49% for DeepSeek 3.1, and 94% for Gemini 2.5 Pro, yielding 59-point separation between first and last and 45-point gap between the top two. In this work we focus on reasoning tasks that demand careful and deep conceptual engagement rather than mere procedural manipulation, and cannot be straightforwardly reduced to off-the-shelf algorithms, whether implemented by external systems or coded by the model itself. This rules out puzzles like Sudoku, Towers of Hanoi, or string manipulation problems, which admit simple algorithmic solutions, and even tasks like propositional satisfiability, which can be readily outsourced to SAT solvers. By contrast, there is no standard algorithm for writing proofs in nontrivial proof systems, and while some of our equational problems could be approached with heuristics like beam search, it is not realistic for model to engineer and execute completeand efficientproof system for such problems on the fly. Even proof checking, though easily solvable by algorithm in principle, would require the one-shot design, construction, and debugging of full software pipeline. Building such system de novo during reasoning task is infeasible. As result, our benchmark compels models to perform genuine logical reasoning instead of relying on canned procedures. Moreover, by centering on proof writing, our tasks more closely reflect the kinds of reasoning challenges that arise in mathematics, science, and engineering, where the construction and justification of arguments is central. This makes PROOFGRID not only more demanding, but also more ecologically valid as test of the reasoning abilities we ultimately want models to develop. Some related work has probed reasoning limits using very large problems or solutions, whose size might approach or exceed model context windows (e.g., problems where the output length grows exponentially with input). With problems of such large sizes, failure is rarely surprising and not very diagnostic, as it is hard to disentangle genuine reasoning shortcomings from token-budget limits, truncation artifacts, or decoding constraints. Moreover, solutions to such problems (e.g., towers of Hanoi) tend to adhere to fixed recursive pattern and thus stress-test symbolic bookkeeping and bandwidth more than they stress-test reasoning. By contrast, both the inputs and the expected outputs of PROOFGRID problems are of relatively small or modest sizes and fit easily within standard context limits, typically occupying only few pages of text. Consequently, model failures cannot be attributed to context truncation or insufficient output budget, but instead reflect an inability to sustain long-horizon symbolic reasoning. Models must discover invariants, manage global constraints, select relevant premises under noise, reconstruct hidden combinatorial structure, and intelligently compose deep and coherent chains of inference, not simply manage context length and repetition. In particular, our main contributions in this paper are the following: We introduce PROOFGRID, rigorous reasoning benchmark that does not equate success with simple answer accuracy but instead requires models to justify and explain their deliberations by producing machine-checkable proofs. The proofs are written in extraordinarily simple formal proof languages (or, as in the case of equational logic, no formal language at all other than simple equations). The benchmark blends: (a) accessible tasks serving as foundational or calibration tests, intended to gauge minimal proof-backed reasoning competence; (b) more difficult tasks that are not yet quite solved by the top 2-3 models but are likely to be relatively soon; (c) frontier challenges that are markedly more difficult than the rest, leaving more headroom for meaningful progress. PROOFGRID adheres to the following design principles: 3 1. With the exception of the frontier challenges, all tasks, including the easier/foundational ones, have sharp disciminating power at the time of writing. This principle relates directly to the issue of benchmark saturation. There are two different notions of saturation, and they are often conflated in the community: ceiling saturation occurs when the strongest 2-3 models start scoring around 95% or higher. By contrast, general or discriminative saturation occurs when most models, not just the elite, start scoring above 90%. These are very different conditions. It is discriminative saturation that renders benchmark obsolete, because it can no longer separate different levels of ability. But as long as task effectively separates respondents of low, medium, and high ability, there is nothing objectionable about the top few models reaching 90% or higher.2 2. Solutions do not rely on content or crystallized mathematical knowledge. 3. Tasks are not readily solvable by code, either by external off-the-shelf solvers or by the models themselves. 4. Tasks do not use excessively long inputs or require excessively long outputs. 5. Tasks do not move up the difficulty ladder by introducing adversarial tricks or obscure rules, or by exploiting model quirks. We use the benchmark to measure and analyze the reasoning performance of wide selection of well-known large models spanning releases from late 2024 until now. Errors in every task are grouped into categories and detailed error statistics per category are given for every model. We quantify each tasks discriminative power using both simple measure we introduce based on Gini mean difference, the discriminative index (DI); as well as new normalized score based on the Fisher information of task items and latent model abilities estimated from task results, using IRT (item response theory). We introduce novel proof-manipulation tasks (proof masking and proof gap-filling) that evaluate models ability to reason explicitly about proofs, reconstruct missing inference steps, and manipulate proof structure. This extends evaluation beyond proof generation and checking to meta-level structural understanding: do models grasp the internal structure of reasoning artifact well enough to manipulate it successfully? Through extensive output analysis, we uncover and document range of widespread behaviors, including selfcontradictory explanations, shifting notions of reasoning correctness, verdict reversals mid-output, and other failure modes. We develop structural framework for comparing reasoning benchmarks along orthogonal axes (input form, output structure, logical expressiveness, inferential depth, and task diversity), and use it to situate PROOFGRID within the broader reasoning-evaluation landscape. We develop an instrumented proof checker that tolerates minor surface-level deviations while explicitly identifying the first substantive error in proof attempt. This allows us to disentangle failures of procedural control from failures of higher-level reasoning, enabling fairer evaluation and substantially richer error analysis than is possible with standard pass/fail checking. We report formal calibration results on the task of equational gap-filling, quantifying how well models selfreported confidence aligns with empirical correctness. Using bin-level calibration gaps and expected calibration error (ECE), we find pervasive miscalibration across many models (with especially pronounced overconfidence), making naive confidence-thresholding unreliable. The resulting calibration benchmarks establish foundation for calibration-aware training and evaluation on proof-manipulation tasks. This separates raw competence from epistemic reliability and yields quantitative yardstick for progress on calibrated reasoning Beyond qualitative analysis and cataloging of reasoning pathologies, we investigate questions of reasoning coherence across different tasks, an analysis that is enabled by the presence of multiple related problems in the benchmark. We give rigorous formalization of notion that we call epistemic stability and provide the first quantitative results capturing the coherence of models reasoning across epistemic contexts, by quantifying the degree of instability between different sets of judgments (such as generated proofs and independent entailment judgments). We show empirically that even top-tier models suffer from high epistemic instability, even when their performance on task may be high in isolation. 2A school exam that gives As to most students would provide very little signal about true ability and would not be useful. But as long as an exam successfully separates students of different skill levels, it should not be dismissed just because few students ace it. (Indeed, the top students are usually expected to earn As, otherwise the exam might be too difficult to function as broad test of competence.) 4 The rest of this paper is structured as follows. Section 2 discusses and organizes related work. Section 3 introduces the PL1 division of PROOFGRID, simple propositional logic, which includes the following tasks (note: tasks whose names end in -c are frontier challenge tasks): 1. PL1-PW (proof writing, discussed in Section 3.1); 2. PL1-PC and PL1-PC-c (proof checking, Section 3.2); 3. PL1-PM and PL1-PM-c (proof masking, Section 4); 4. PL1-GF and PL1-GF-c (proof gap-filling, Section 5). Section 6 introduces the PL2 division, with only one task, PL2-PW. Section 7 describes the PL3 division, with two tasks, PL3-PW and PL3-PC. Section 9 introduces PL4, comprising two tasks PL4-PW and PL4-PW-c. Section 9.3 presents the equational-reasoning tasks (EQ-PC, EQ-ER, and EQ-GF) and corresponding results. Section 8 formalizes notion of epistemic stability and presents related results, while Section 10 concludes. Appendix defines NDL, naturaldeduction proof language widely used in PROOFGRID, Appendix discusses IRT and its application to PROOFGRID, while Appendix specifies various technical/operational details.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Reasoning has emerged as core yardstick for assessing the performance of language models. However, there are many types of reasoning and many different research directions exploring the intersection of reasoning with AI models. In this section we aim to clarify this complicated and fragmented landscape, and explain where our own work fits into the broader picture. While we try to be comprehensive, our primary focus in this paper is deductive reasoningthe bedrock of mathematics. Although copious and significant research exists on other forms of reasoning also (induction, causality, and abduction, to name few), deduction has historically dominated the literature on AI and reasoning. Because of the central role that deductive reasoning has played in the field, and its prominence in PROOFGRID, it will be helpful to clarify an aspect of it that is sometimes misunderstood. Deduction is often said to be non-ampliative species of reasoning, meaning that there is no information in the conclusion that is not, in some sense, already contained in the premises. This supposedly contrasts with other types of reasoning, such as induction, where the conclusion conveys information beyond what is found in the premises. But this is somewhat crude distinction that can be misleading, even though it is common both in and out of the AI community (e.g., it is embraced in the HELM frameworks discussion of reasoning evaluation [Liang et al. 2023, p. 38]). It is true, of course, that every interpretation which satisfies the premises of valid deductive argument also satisfies the conclusion, and in that strict model-theoretic sense, the conclusion cannot say anything that is not already forced by the premises. So the claim that deduction is not ampliative is true enough in the narrow technical sense of truth-conditional semantics. But it breaks down in several important respects, particularly when examined from epistemic, computational, or representational perspectives. Epistemically and cognitively, deduction routinely yields vast amounts of information, particularly in the mathematical sciences, revealing structure, compressing information, providing explanations and warrants, and connecting distant concepts. It would be untenable to say that Euclids theorems dont provide any information that is not already contained in his postulates, or that Fermats last theorem doesnt tell us anything that is not already in the axioms of ZFC. Before the proof by Andrew Wiles was published in 1995, we didnt even know whether Fermats last theorem was true; we only suspected that it was. It was the deductive reasoning of Wiles that provided justification for believing that the theorem is true, explained why it is true, connected it to elliptic curves and modular forms, and introduced powerful new techniques and rich conceptual structures. In general, the distinction between ampliative and non-ampliative inference is much more fragile than usually assumed, as is the very distinction between deductive and nondeductive arguments. The topic is too complex to discuss in depth here, but it is worth noting that expert opinions on the subject run wide gamut, with some argumentation theorists (who study everyday natural-language argumentation and reasoning), such as Groarke [1999], claiming that all natural-language reasoning is deductive, and that all arguments should be understood as attempts at deductive arguments. While we do not quite endorse this strong thesis here, it is important to understand that there are compelling arguments for it. In the context of AI benchmarks, the position is perhaps easier to understand when one considers problems in datasets such as MATH, like problem asking for the roots of quadratic equation or the area of rectangular field. Strictly speaking, numeric answers like {2, 5} for the equation roots or 138 for the rectangle area are not formally entailed by the problem 5 context, because the context omits the necessary mathematical background: definitions (what is root, what is rectangle, etc.), relevant theorems and results (the quadratic formula, area formulas, . . .), and so on. Nevertheless, the underlying reasoning remains entirely deductive. The abridged problem formulation doesnt shift the reasoning into the domain of abduction or induction, nor does it make the answer ampliative. It simply reflects the economy of expression in our representations of mathematical reasoning. Philosophers of science such as Musgrave [2003] have defended equally strong positions regarding deduction in scientific reasoning, claiming, for example, that the only valid arguments are deductively valid arguments, and that deductive logic is the only logic that we have or need [Musgrave 2012] and that instances of inductive or abductive reasoning are really enthymematic deductions. Indeed, one could plausibly argue that even in what appear to be inherently inductive settings, such as applications of statistical techniques that seem to go beyond what has been strictly observed, the reasoning often remains primarily deductive. In Bayesian confirmation theory, for example, the move from prior to posterior is, mathematically speaking, an application of the probability axioms, and the update itself is deduction made inside richer calculus. So what might look ampliative in bare propositional language is non-ampliative once the relevant probabilistic assumptions are made explicit in the model specification.3 Likewise, deduction plays key role in empirical-error-minimization methods like regression or SVM, or indeed any technique that can be viewed through the lens of statistical learning theory (SLT). This is not just because these techniques are deductively analyzed and proven to minimize error, but also because when we apply model to new input that we have not seen before, the reasoning is often best understood as deduction. The point can be illustrated with ordinary linear regression, especially when formulated in Vapniks SLT framework. Suppose we fix hypothesis class of linear functions fβ(x) = β0 + βx, loss function such as squared error, and learning rule that selects from the function minimizing empirical risk, perhaps with an added regularization term to control capacity. Suppose further that we assume the training sample and previously unseen instance are drawn i.i.d. from common underlying distribution, that the feature representation is stable across train and test cases, and that the target relation is appropriately approximated within the chosen hypothesis class. Then, given the observed data, it follows deductively which parameter vector minimizes the specified objective, which hypothesis is therefore selected by the learning rule, what prediction that hypothesis assigns to new input, and (given the SLT machinery) what kinds of bounds relate empirical risk to expected risk as function of sample size and hypothesis-class capacity. On this picture, the reasoning is not an irreducibly ampliative leap from observed to unobserved cases. Rather, it is chain of deductions within richer premise set that includes the hypothesis space, the loss function, the sampling assumptions, and the generalization theory. The genuine uncertainty lies not in the mathematical derivation of the fitted line or the associated risk bound, but in whether the assumptions underwriting that derivation are good assumptions for the problem at hand. But that is business as usual for deduction, which distinguishes between validity and soundness. Whether the premises entail the conclusion is one thing, and logic can tell us that; whether the premises are true (e.g., whether the test instances are indeed drawn i.i.d. from common underlying distribution) is up to the way the world is and must be settled by empirical investigation. In any event, whether all reasoning is ultimately deductive (or whether it can be fruitfully reconstructed as such) is complex and contentious question, and we do not aim to take definitive position here. Our more limited point is that the distinction between ampliative and non-ampliative reasoning (and the characterization of deduction as non-ampliative) is of questionable stability and value; and that, when scrutinized, many allegedly ampliative cases of inference are far more deductive in structure than the standard contrast suggests. In what follows we begin with broad overview of reasoning datasets, grouped into four categories: natural-language reasoning; mathematical and scientific reasoning; generalist reasoning; and formal reasoning. We then introduce set of analytical dimensions that provide finer-grained understanding of this landscape, and use these axes to classify broad range of benchmarks, including PROOFGRID. The section concludes with brief comparison of PROOFGRID and other major datasets. 3Of course, Bayesian updating by itself does not resolve the further questions of which prior to adopt, which likelihood is appropriate, or which model family to use. But that says nothing about the indispensability of induction. To make that stronger claim, one would need to show that the reasoning by which such choices are made is itself irreducibly nondeductive, rather than merely dependent on substantive assumptions or principles that are not fixed by logic alone. Uncertainty or underdetermination at the level of premises does not entail the presence of any distinctively inductive inferential mechanisms. Such uncertainty is manifested even in mathematics, e.g., consider the historical doubts regarding Euclids fifth postulate, the axiom of choice, the continuum hypothesis and large-cardinal axioms, and even the law of the excluded middle and double negation elimination. 6 2.1 Birds-Eye Survey 2.1.1 Natural-Language Reasoning Within the larger NLP community, major thread of work involving AI models and reasoning investigates the ability of such models to perform natural-language inference (NLI), and particularly their ability to determine whether set of natural-language sentences entails target sentence. The general problem is this: Given set of sentences (or more generally, text corpus) and hypothesis (representing, for example, potential answer to question) determine whether = h, i.e., whether entails h. (1) Common alternative terms for include context, theory, knowledge base, or simply the set of premises, while the sentence is also referred to as theputativeconclusion, and sometimes as the target sentence or the query. Note that entailment here is usually (but not always, see below) semantic, it is not formal or strictly logical entailment. In formal logic, if entails then it does so purely by virtue of the syntactic form of the sentences in and h, not by virtue of the meanings of the words in those sentences. We thus say that Tom is tall and thin formally entails Tom is tall because the entailment has nothing to do with what tall and thin actually mean. We could replace those adjectives with any others and the entailment would still hold. In fact, that is how formal entailment is defined: We quantify over all possible interpretations of the extra-logical symbols, and we say that no matter how you interpret Tom, tall, and thin, i.e., for any possible way of assigning meanings (denotations) to those terms, the conclusion comes out true whenever the premises are true. By contrast, the usual textbook definition of semantic entailment is this: = iff the truth of the sentences in guarantees or makes certain the truth of h, or equivalently, iff the falsity of guarantees or makes certain the falsity of at least one element of . However, this common textbook gloss is loaded with subjective psychological notions like certainty. crisper definition of semantic entailment can be given via possible worlds: entails iff in every possible world (or model) where all sentences in are true, is also true. This sounds very much like the definition of entailment in formal logic, but there is crucial difference. In this definition we do not quantify over completely arbitrary interpretations of non-logical symbols. Instead, those meanings are (largely) fixed. Proper names like Tom, for example, are fixed: they are rigid designators that pick out the same constant element across all possible worlds. Lexical items that are not proper names (say, words like politician and smoker) are allowed some degree of cross-world variation but only in the sense that the sets of individuals answering to these descriptions (the extensions of these words) may vary from world to world, as one world may have different set of politicians from another, and someone who smokes in this world might not smoke in another. But the concepts expressed by these words are uniform across all possible worlds.4 So the proper way to understand semantic entailment is this: once we fix the meanings of the extra-logical words Tom, thin, and tall, and overweight, in any possible world in which Tom is thin is true, Tom is not overweight is also true. Thus, Tom is thin semantically entails Tom is not overweight, even though it does not formally entail it. Sometimes the entailment is neither formal nor semantic but rather one of implicature. Here is textbook example: Some students managed to pass the exam strongly suggests that some students did not pass the exam, and so in an NLP context we might say that Some students did not pass the exam is entailed by Some students managed to pass the exam, even though it is neither semantically nor formally entailed by it. It is possible to tease out number of other commonsensical types of entailment that are disjoint from the above kinds, but ultimately, entailment judgments in NLP are made empirically; labeled examples are annotated based on what typical annotators perceive to be consequences of given context . This purely descriptive framingentailment by democracy, so to speakis reasonable if the goal is to study entailment as an empirical phenomenon, as it functions in everyday language use. But it does not offer normative basis for adjudicating the correctness of an inference, and this may have implications for models trained on such data. The heuristics-and-biases literature has documented vast range of systematic reasoning fallacies to which most people (including reasoning experts) are susceptible [Tversky and Kahneman 1974]. If what counts as entailment becomes conflated with what is commonly perceived as such, even when these perceptions are flawed, model could score well on such benchmarks by mimicking incorrect or biased reasoning patterns. Problem (1) originated under the name RTE (Recognizing Textual Entailment).5 The first RTE challenge was launched in 2005 [Dagan et al. 2009]. The fourth RTE challenge, in 2008, introduced an alternative and more general formulation of 4See, for instance, Heim and Kratzer [1998, Sec. 12.3]. 5The term NLI started gaining wider currency after MacCartneys influential 2009 PhD dissertation [MacCartney 2009]. 7 the problem: Given and h, decide whether entails h, contradicts p, or leaves the status of unknowncorresponding to the labels entailment, contradiction, and neutral. This three-way setup was later adopted and popularized by large-scale benchmarks such as SNLI [Bowman et al. 2015] and MNLI [Williams et al. 2018], helping to standardize the task in modern NLP. Historically, NLI work studied real-world natural language, usually drawn from naturally occurring or crowdsourced text. The context was typically single sentence or short paragraph at most, and the emphasis was on lexical, syntactic, and pragmatic nuances (arising, e.g., from various types of implicatures) in the relevant sentences. As already mentioned, entailment was typically defined informally,6 although logic-based techniques were also used from the beginning [Bos and Markert 2005, Akhmatova and Mollá 2005], albeit with underwhelming results [Bos and Markert 2006]. Early logic-based efforts still dealt with short sentence pairs drawn from real-world contexts and attended closely to lexical and syntactic features (captured, e.g., by wide-coverage parsers), but they grounded entailment in logic and formal semantic representations, such as those of DRT (Discourse Representation Theory) [Kamp 1981] or MRS (Minimal Recursion Semantics) [Copestake et al. 2005]. So even within mainstream NLI, there was always thread of research that treated entailment as rigorously defined, logic-driven relation, but applied to ecologically authentic and linguistically rich text. However, the limited success of that approach in early NLI made way for statistical models with much more shallow representations that nonetheless proved more effective in practice. There is relatively newer strand of NLI work that is more explicitly inspired by and tied to formal logic, exemplified by datasets like RuleTaker [Clark et al. 2021] and systems like ProofWriter [Tafjord et al. 2021]. The main difference of these approaches from traditional NLI is that their data sources are expressed in controlled natural language (CNL), or quasi-natural language, usually templated English sentences synthetically generated from formal logical data with the help of grammars.7 Because the starting point is synthetically generated formal dataset,8 it can be adjusted to support experiments with deeper reasoning and precisely defined entailment conditions, often grounded in subsets of logic such as stratified Datalog or various description logics. These datasets emphasize multi-step inference chains, closed-world reasoning, and deterministic label generation and evaluation, enabling fine-grained control over complexity and semantic scope. While the language is less naturalistic and rather inauthentic, the trade-off is more transparent link between linguistic input and symbolic inference, so these datasets have been more widely used to probe the reasoning abilities of neural models. Besides RuleTaker [Clark et al. 2021] and ProofWriter [Tafjord et al. 2021], datasets in this category include ProntoQA [Saparov and He 2023], LogicInference [Ontañón et al. 2022], LogicAsker [Wan et al. 2024], LogicNLI [Tian et al. 2021], and FLD [Morishita et al. 2023]. Folio [Han et al. 2024] can arguably be placed in the same group. Although its sentences are not programmatically derived from logical forms, they are nevertheless synthetic insofar as they do not occur naturally; they were manually created by human annotators based on randomly selected Wikipedia pages. The resulting style is conspicuously stilted in the interest of enabling straightforward mappings to formal (FOL) notation.9 In general, NLI-oriented datasets (certainly those from the mainstream NLI tradition, but to lesser extent those of the CNL variety as well), tend to focus on the inferences that are found in natural language, which are characterized by relatively shallow deductions, are strongly impacted by linguistic nuances, and incorporate commonsenseand often non-monotonicreasoning principles such as the closed-world assumption and negation-as-failure. In this setting, AI models are viewed as soft reasoners [Clark et al. 2021] or soft theorem provers, simulating logical reasoning using natural language. By contrast, we will see that mathand logic-oriented datasets like PROOFGRID are usually expressed in formal symbolic notation and focus on long chains of deductions whose correctness is underwritten by strict logical and mathematical norms. They gauge the degree to which AI models can serve as hard reasoners or theorem provers for logic and mathematics. 6Manning [2006, p.2] argued early on that textual inference task should adopt as the standard of inference what human would be happy to infer from piece of text. In particular, items would be assessed by people that are awake, careful, moderately intelligent and informed, and with reasonable document interpretation expertise, but not by semanticists or similar academics [our emphasis]. These people would use whatever background and real world knowledge that they usually bring to interpreting texts. 7Representative examples include Dave is green, Bob is round, Big people are rough, and If someone is young and round then they are kind. The extent to which these may be viewed as natural language inference problems is debatable. 8This is typically set of pairs (S, p) consisting of collection of formal sentences and single sentence pthe same type of objects that we call arguments in PROOFGRID. (These pairs are known as sequents in logic, but we avoid that term here because it tends to be associated with Gentzen-style proof systems, which are not our focus in this paper.) 9A representative FOLIO problem features the hypothesis/conclusion Sea eel is an eel, while the corresponding context consists of the following sentences: All eels are fish. No fish are plants. thing is either plant or animal. Nothing that breathes is paper. All animals breathe. If sea eel is either an eel or plant, then sea eel is an eel or an animal. 8 While much of the work on natural-language reasoning centers around entailment, whether formally or informally defined, there are other reasoning tasks that depart from this framework. For instance, some benchmarks focus on abductive reasoning, where, given natural-language context, the goal is to select the most plausible explanation or continuation (from number of candidates). representative example is HellaSwag [Zellers et al. 2019], in which models must choose the most plausible ending to scenario from set of options. Although this task is not about entailment, solving it requires integrating background knowledge, commonsense expectations, and causal plausibility, all of which can be said to involve (informal) inference. These benchmarks therefore probe reasoning in broader and more intuitive sense, often drawing on patterns learned from real-world event sequences rather than strict logical inference. In contrast to deduction-focused benchmarks, the reasoning here is contextual, gradient, and plausibility-based, highlighting the diversity of what reasoning can mean in NLP. 2.1.2 Mathematical and Scientific Reasoning Another class of benchmarks regularly invoked in conversations about AI models and reasoning comprises tests specifically designed to evaluate mathematical competence. Here the inputs are math problems (from various branches of mathematics and levels of difficulty) and the outputs are the respective answers. Unlike NLI, the focus is on longer chains of deductions whose correctness is underwritten by strict and explicitly formulated logical and mathematical norms, not by tacit informal conventions or pragmatics. The idea is to gauge the extent to which models can be used as hard logico-mathematical problem solvers. Representative samples include GSM8K [Cobbe et al. 2021], MATH [Hendrycks et al. 2021b], OlympiadBench [He et al. 2024], Omni-MATH [Bofei et al. 2024], and the more demanding and recent OlymMATH [Sun et al. 2025] and FrontierMath [Glazer et al. 2024]. The problems in these benchmarks are expressed in natural language and the expected answers are what we might call literals, typically numbers or symbolic expressions like x2 5. Models are expected to output their final answers in special designated format, usually within the braces of LATEX command like boxed{ }. Questions are culled from many sources: standardized school curricula from various countries (such as K-12 textbooks); statewide and national exams (SATs, ACT, AP Calculus and Statistics, CBSE/ICSE/ISC exams in India, etc.); math competitions and Olympiads (AMC, Putnam, AIME, USAMO, IMO, and so on); materials from educational organizations like AoPS (the Art of Problem Solving); undergraduate math problem sets (e.g., MIT OCW, Harvard, etc.); crowdsourcing; and synthetic data as well. Some of the more recent math benchmarks are remarkably challenging. FRONTIERMATH [Glazer et al. 2024], for example, introduced in late 2024 by the research institute Epoch AI with the explicit aim of evaluating AI models on advanced mathematics, comprises hundreds of original research-level problems across wide range of domains, such as algebraic geometry, number theory, and category theory. These problems were created by expert mathematicians, vetted for originality, and selected for their theoretical depth and difficulty. The creators note that even seasoned mathematicians may require hours or days to solve many of them. According to Epoch AI, as of September 2025, no model reached mean accuracy of 25% on FRONTIERMATH,10 though GPT-5 came close at 24.83%, with OpenAIs o4-mini at second place, followed by Gemini 2.5 Pro, Grok-4, and o3.11 By early March 2026, the top performer was GPT-5.2, at 31.3%. However, regardless of their difficulty level, the extent to which these datasets actually test mathematical reasoning is not obvious. They certainly dont do so in an explicit sense, given that they follow an outcome-based evaluation paradigm. That is, models are judged on whether they produce the correct final answer, typically single literal value, without requiring them to provide logically correct arguments in support of those answers.12 This is rather incongruous with the subject matter, because, since the time of Euclid, mathematics has valued proof over mere assertion. But if performance is gauged only by inspecting the final answer, models may get full credit even if they arrive at the correct result through 10In December 2024, OpenAI claimed that o3 attained an accuracy in excess of 25%, but that claim has been controversial and Epoch AI was not able to reproduce that figure. 11These results are for the easier tiers, 13. The hardest tier is 4, currently led by GPT-5 with 8.33%. 12COT (chain-of-thought) prompting is widely used for these tests, and model outputs are (potentially long) sequences of intermediate steps culminating in final boxed{ } answer. However, this is done mostly in order to improve the question-answering performance of the models, not to evaluate the reasoning contained in the COT traces. Model performance is still judged only on whether the final answer matches the ground truth. What little preliminary work has been done in this direction so far has found that there exists significant disparity between answer accuracy and faithfulness of the CoT reasoning generated by LLMs, indicating that they often arrive at correct answers through incorrect reasoning [Nguyen et al. 2024]. Some newer datasets, such as NuminaMath, include certain proportion of proof-based problems [Li et al. 2024, p. 12], but they do not currently support proof evaluation. 9 memorization, pattern-matching, heuristic shortcuts, or statistical artifacts rather than sound reasoning. Benchmark creators do attempt to guard against guesswork (e.g., through guessproofness constraints [Glazer et al. 2024, p. 3] and large or intricate answers), but these measures are only partial defenses against the core issue: These benchmarks dont verify whether the model arrived at the right solution for the right reasons.13 related issue with benchmarks in this category, at least as diagnostic tools for reasoning, is that they inevitably favor models with vast stores of crystallized mathematical knowledge. Large language models are trained on enormous corpora, including textbooks, research articles, lecture notes, problem banks, explanations, proofs, tutorials, and forums like StackExchange, giving them access to breadth and depth of mathematical content far beyond what any human mathematician could hope to internalize. This gives AI models significant advantage. Problems are built around familiar mathematical objects (polynomials, inequalities, divisibility conditions, geometric relations, and so on), where the underlying concepts, as well as common tricks and transformations, are very well-represented in the training data. Even when problems are novel or cleverly constructed or transformed, the models ability to draw on tremendous body of heuristics and solution templates might enable them to perform relatively well without necessarily engaging in deep reasoning, aided mostly by pattern recognition, recall, and analogy. This is especially true if the problems are heavily computational, as is the case with many of these benchmarks (including FRONTIERMATH), which allows the models to leverage their coding skills. By contrast, as mentioned in the introduction, PROOFGRID is deliberately designed to neutralize this advantage. Its problems are fully symbolic, devoid of meaningful names or context, and grounded in logic systems that offer no opportunity to exploit real-world mathematical knowledge. It thus forces models to rely primarily on fluid reasoning, by constructing and verifying proofs from abstract premises, using inferential strategies that are common in mathematical practice. This offers clearer lens into models true reasoning abilities, unassisted by prior knowledge. similar class of benchmarks target scientific problem solving. historically important sample is the AI2 Reasoning Challenge (ARC) benchmark [Clark et al. 2018], introduced by the Allen Institute for AI and aiming to evaluate understanding and reasoning in AI models within the domain of elementary and middle school science. It contains 7,787 natural-language multiple-choice questions sourced from publicly available U.S. standardized science exams and split into two subsets: ARC-Easy: Questions that can often be answered by surface-level lexical cues or information retrieval techniques. ARC-Challenge: curated subset of 2,590 questions that are supposed to defeat retrieval-based methods and are intended to test deeper forms of scientific and commonsense reasoning. The questions span broad range of science topics typically covered in grades 39, including the life sciences (biology, ecosystems, nutrition, human body systems, etc.); physical science (forces and motion, energy, matter, electricity); earth and space science (geology, weather, solar system); and scientific method and reasoning (experimental design, measurement, cause-effect relationships, and so on). The benchmark was explicitly designed to resist shallow statistical methods and require multi-step inference, background knowledge, and commonsense reasoning. At the time of its release in 2018, ARC represented significant step towards evaluating genuine science-related reasoning ability in language models, but by now it has been effectively saturated, with many models achieving superhuman performance even on the Challenge subset. ARC has been superseded by the science-focused parts of relatively more recent benchmarks like BBH and Multi-MLU, although these have also been largely saturated by now. ARC and related science-focused benchmarks bear directly on our earlier remarks regarding the distinction between deductive and nondeductive reasoning, raising important questions about how deduction should be understood and identified. Consider this ARC problem: student riding bicycle observes that it moves faster on smooth road than on rough road. This happens because the smooth road has: (A) less gravity (B) more gravity (C) less friction [correct] (D) more friction. This may appear to rely on simple empirical knowledge and commonsense reasoning. But if modelor humanhas internalized appropriate generalizations like Friction impedes motion and Rough surfaces increase friction, then correct answer may be reached through chain of deterministic (and arguably deductive) reasoning. From this perspective, the distinction between deductive and nondeductive reasoning may depend less on the task formulation and more on the implicit premises available to the test taker. This ambiguity reinforces the need for benchmarks that 13While acknowledging the technical difficulty of FRONTIERMATH, number of Fields Medalists pointed out that the numerical format of the questions feels somewhat contrived, with Richard Borcherds remarking, in bit of understatement, that these benchmark problems arent quite the same as coming up with original proofs [Glazer et al. 2024, p. 11]. 10 make the role of deduction explicit and verifiablewhere the logical structure of the argument can be scrutinized and the conclusions validity traced to its premises. Finally, the same issues we discussed in the context of math benchmarks apply here as well. The actual inferences of the model are not tested, only its final answer. Accordingly, these benchmarks do not directly test reasoning either, but rather question-answering ability. Likewise, crystallized knowledge plays crucial role in successful performance in these benchmarks, and can be confounding factor in assessing the extent to which models arrive at their answers through genuine reasoning rather than recall or statistical association. The exploitation of shallow statistical patterns has been repeatedly demonstrated for earlier and smaller transformer models, such as BERT, even on relatively abstract logical reasoning problems expressed in natural language, with, e.g., Zhang et al. [2023, 2] concluding that instead of learning to emulate the correct reasoning function, BERT has in fact learned statistical features that inherently exist in logical reasoning problems. But similar concerns arise for newer, larger, and more powerful models. For example, Nguyen et al. [2024] evaluated 5 families of instruction-tuned causal LLMs ranging from 7B to 175B parameters, including an early version of ChatGPT, on two multi-hop QA benchmarks. They found that the models often make the correct guess for the wrong reasons when they are asked to think step-by-step, showing significant gap (around 16 percentage points) between answer correctness and COT correctness and concluding that The correct final answer may not necessarily follow from faithful reasoning. We observe significant gap between answer accuracy and reasoning faithfulness. It highlights the necessity of directly evaluating the reasoning steps rather than solely scoring the final answers. This is confirmed by our results, e.g., models do very well when we ask them to simply guess whether proof is correct, but when we ask them to justify their answers with details, performance drops significantly (see Table 6). However, Nguyen et al. [2024] also reported that the performance gap between the final answer and reasoning worsens as the model size increases. As the answer accuracy also increases with the model size, it suggests that the bigger models may have the knowledge of the final answer without the need to perform reasoning. By contrast, we find that the gap shrinks for the best and largest models, because they rely less on superficial patterns and more on honest reasoning toil. The methodological issue of assessing models indirectly, by their final answers only, extends to frontier models and to the one domain that is supposed to be the epitome of abstract deductive reasoning: mathematics. While AI models have undeniably made great strides here, the recent buzz about such models being able to solve Olympiad-level math problems is premature. recent manual analysis of 455 carefully curated shortlist IMO (International Math Olympiad) problems from 2009 to 2023 found that frontier models often generate incorrect solutions, that is, solutions that contain reasoning errors despite getting the final answer right. Specifically, once the models full reasoning was taken into account, the study found that the conditional probability of an answer being fully correct given that the final answer was correct was 12.5% for o1, 14.3% for o3-mini, and rather astounding 0% for o1-mini, DeepSeek R1, and Gemini 2.0 [Mahdavi et al. 2025, p. 8]. The authors write that current LLMs fall significantly short of solving challenging Olympiad-level problems and frequently fail to distinguish correct mathematical reasoning from clearly flawed solutions. Our analyses demonstrate that the occasional correct final answers provided by LLMs often result from pattern recognition or heuristic shortcuts rather than genuine mathematical reasoning. These findings underscore the substantial gap between LLM performance and human expertise in advanced mathematical reasoning and highlight the importance of developing benchmarks that prioritize the soundness of the reasoning used to arrive at an answer rather than the mere correctness of the final answers. 2.1.3 Generalist/Hybrid Reasoning In this category we find more recent and wider-ranging benchmarks that touch on reasoning somewhat indirectly or only in part, even though they often end up informing public claims about the reasoning abilities of AI models. Notable examples include BBH [Suzgun et al. 2023] and its successor BBEH (Big Bench Extra Hard) [Google Research 2025], MMLU [Hendrycks et al. 2021a] and its successor MMLU Pro [Wang et al. 2024], HLE (Humanitys Last Exam) [Scale AI and Center for AI Safety 2025], SuperGPQA [Team et al. 2025]. These benchmarks aim to evaluate models across wide array of subjects and tasks, many of which do involve reasoning to some degree. These tasks range from math and logic puzzles to legal analysis, ethics scenarios, high-school and college science, the social sciences, professional/standardized exams (SAT, GRE, LSAT, etc.), and so on. In some cases, the required inference resembles formal deduction (e.g., logic grid puzzles or syllogisms); in others, the inference relies on background knowledge, commonsense, or pragmatic reasoning. Although these benchmarks provide valuable broad-strokes view of model capabilities, they offer relatively coarsegrained signal about reasoning, as they conflate different cognitive demands (e.g., domain knowledge, reading comprehension, cultural context, symbolic manipulation, and so on). Moreover, the evaluation remains outcome-based, judging models only on their final answers without assessing whether those answers are supported by correct or coherent reasoning 11 steps. As such, these benchmarks complement more focused and diagnostic tools that aim to isolate and evaluate more precisely defined forms of reasoning. well-known benchmark that can be placed in this category, motivated in part by the same considerations as PROOFGRID, is the Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus for Artificial General Intelligence (ARC-AGI, or simply ARC), introduced by Chollet [2019]. It was designed to measure human-like fluid intelligence, rather than the crystallized intelligence typically assessed by standard AI benchmarks. Because of its prominence, the rest of this section is devoted to detailed examination of ARC. ARC comprises few-shot visual grid-transformation tasks, where the test taker must conjecture an underlying pattern from small number of input-output examples and successfully apply it to novel test grid. The tasks are exclusively visual, strictly precluding not just domain-specific knowledge but also language, with the aim of relying only on fundamental core knowledge priors [Spelke and Kinzler 2007] like objects, numbers, actions, and space (or basic geometry).14 Humans are generally able to solve ARC tasks without much difficulty. As models scaled and researchers began applying massive test-time compute and brute-force search techniques to the original dataset, ARC-AGI-2 was introduced in early 2025 to re-establish rigorous capability boundary [Chollet et al. 2026]. ARC-AGI-2 retains the visual grid format of the original ARC-AGI (which has come to be known as ARC-AGI-1) but introduces more complex tasks (requiring, for example, sequence of object-manipulating actions that must respect multiple rules and contextual constraints). These new tasks are not as easily solved as those in ARC-AGI-1, but humans still succeed with probability 66% when attempting to solve randomly sampled task from the new benchmark [Chollet et al. 2026][p. 7]. key aim of ARC, which is shared by PROOFGRID, is to minimize the memorization and data-retrieval advantages of LLMs, and to test fluid problem-solving ability. But ARC goes further by precluding language and keeping the tasks strictly visual, with the goal of testing whether models can solve these tasks using only the few foundational priors that seem available to humans, roughly those singled out by core-knowledge theorists. Chollet speaks of test of human-like general intelligence and writes that an actionable test of general intelligence that would be fair for both humans and machines should only feature tasks that assume the four core knowledge systems listed above, and should not involve any acquired knowledge outside of these priors [Chollet 2019][p. 27]. ARC is an elegant benchmark that has been influential in the field, but several issues are worth noting. First, ARC claims to be measure of general fluid intelligence, but as was acknowledged by Chollet [2019][p. 54], no empirical evidence has been presented in support of that contention, e.g., no evidence that performance on ARC correlates meaningfully with gF , or even with g.15 This is not implausible, given that ARC has qualitative similarities to well-known nonverbal intelligence tests like RPM (Ravens Progressive Matrices [McCallum 2003][ch. 11]), and the latter have been psychometrically validated (e.g., RPM is known to load fairly strongly on g, although see Gignac [2015] for recently discovered limitations). But superficial task similarity is not enough. Psychometrically, two tasks can look alike and yet differ lot in what drives performance: visuospatial search, working-memory load, perceptual segmentation, task-format familiarity, responsegeneration demands, and so on. ARC, in particular (and especially ARC-AGI-2), differs from RPM in significant ways: it is open-ended rather than multiple-choice, it requires constructing an output rather than recognizing one, it imposes heavier search demands, and uses pixel-grid micro-world with its own idiosyncratic priors. Such differences could substantially 14According to the work of developmental psychologist Spelke [2000] (see also Spelke and Kinzler [2007]), human cognition starts with small set of evolutionarily old, domain-specific representational systems, the four main ones being objects, numbers, actions, and space (a fifth domain-specific ability, for social interaction, is acknowledged as distinct possibility). These systems are supposed to appear early in development, be shared in some form with other animals, and provide the foundations for all subsequent learning. The core-knowledge thesis is clearly nativist, or at least anti-blank-slate, but it differs in one major respect from the more conventional nativism of Chomsky and Fodor: the role of language. The latter posit language as the most highly distinctive computational system of the human mind, set apart by its uniqueness, its abstract combinatorial structure, and its explanatory power. Core knowledge theorists, by contrast, tend to view language as powerful add-ona coordinator and amplifier of more basic cognitive systems that predate human language, insofar as they appear in infants before the development of language, and even in other animals. However, many cognitive scientists would maintain that language is so transformative that it cannot be understood as just an important add-on; on this view, language doesnt just organize or decorate cognition, it fundamentally reorganizes it. 15The famous g-factor (the stands for general intelligence), introduced by Spearman [1904], is latent factor inferred from positive correlations among diverse cognitive test scores (people who do well on one kind of mental task tend to do well on others, on average). It is statistical construct from psychometrics. Spearman believed that everyone has relatively fixed intellectual capacity, core mental ability that plays role in virtually every cognitive task, and that the g-factor measured precisely this ability. Gf (fluid intelligence) is related factor, representing the capacity to reason and solve novel problems independently of previously acquired knowledge. In psychometric models [Cattell 1943, 1963, Carroll 1993], it is typically distinguished from crystallized intelligence (Gc), which reflects knowledge and skills acquired through learning and experience. 12 increase or decrease correlation with g. So while there is plausible case for expecting some positive correlation between ARC and Gv16, and perhaps even with or Gf , to the best of our knowledge that remains an open empirical question. Second, barring the use of language in general test of intelligence (especially one that is routinely applied to language models) seems questionable, both on priori and on empirical grounds. Language alone does not give models an unfair advantage. Language is faculty that all neurobiologically normal humans share. It is not, by itself, crystallized intelligence, nor is it bias; it is key innate mental capacity that cognitive science recognizes as central to thought. It is particularly important for the most prominent forms of human reasoning, from argumentation in politics and everyday life to scientific and mathematical reasoning, which have been deeply intertwined with language17 for thousands of years, especially with reason giving. The relevant word in ancient Greek is logos, rich term that can mean speech, word, account, explanation, or argumentall of them inherently linguistic objects. Likewise for ratio, the Latin root of reasoning, which also revolves around giving an account, explaining, reckoning, and justifying. These are the core practices of both everyday reasoning as well as mathematical and scientific reasoning, where one articulates premises and derives conclusions, makes and justifies claims and other inferential commitments, and explains why something is the case. The importance of language is widely recognized in psychometric theories of intelligence and testing, even by advocates of nonverbal tests like Wechsler, who as far back as 1939 was stressing the need to include both verbal and nonverbal tasks in intelligence tests, since doing so makes possible comparison between subjects facility in using words and symbols and his ability to manipulate objects, and to perceive visual patterns [Wechsler 1939][p. 146]. He also made the empirical observation that Subjects of superior intelligence generally do better on the verbal, and subjects of inferior intelligence do better on the performance (nonverbal) part of the examination [p. 147]. critical finding [Lakin and Kell 2020][p. 531] of what is perhaps the most prominent and widely accepted [McGrew et al. 2023] scientific theory of intelligence, the CHC (Cattell-Horn-Carroll) theory [McGrew 2009], is that the general reasoning factor (Gf ) may be decomposed into subfactors: (1) deductive reasoning (termed sequential reasoning by Carroll and largely comprising verbal tasks), (2) quantitative reasoning (inductive or deductive reasoning with quantitative concepts), and (3) inductive reasoning (often measured with figural tasks; Carroll, 1993). good reasoning test, then, should probably measure all three of these reasoning factors or at least not be strongly biased toward one (Wilhelm, 2005). This fact is commonly overlooked in studies that represent fluid reasoning abilities with single figural reasoning test, such as Ravens Progressive Matrices test (Raven, Court, & Raven, 1977). [Lakin and Kell 2020][p. 531] Like RPM, ARC only taps subfactor (3) above, and even there we would claim that serious inductive reasoning (as done, e.g., in science) almost always involves language and the explicit articulation of hypotheses and evidence. The importance of language is also recognized by rival theories to CHC, such as BIS (the Berlin Intelligence Structure) [Beauducel and Kersting 2002], which holds that measuring reasoning requires tasks that sample across the main content areas, of which there are three: verbal, numeric, and figural; and suggests that the diversity of content required to measure Gf consistently will require going beyond the figural tasks (e.g., Ravens Progressive Matrices) [Lakin and Kell 2020][p. 532]. Likewise, recent work on the limitations of exclusively figural tasks like RPM advises that researchers are discouraged to include only Ravens in an investigation, if valid estimate of is sought. [Gignac 2015][p. 71]. ARC also aims to be test of abstraction, but it is important to be precise about what sort of abstraction mechanisms are tapped by nonverbal tests. Cognitive science has traditionally distinguished between two types of abstraction: low-level perceptual abstraction, which allows us to recognize objects across viewpoints, strip away visual noise to see that two shapes are both triangles, and so on; and 16In the Cattell-Horn-Carroll family of psychometric theories of intelligence [Carroll 1993], the Gv factor captures the ability to perceive, analyze, manipulate, and think with visual patterns and spatial information. Walrath et al. [2020] write that Gv involves range of visual processes, ranging from fairly simple visual perceptual tasks to higher-level, visual, cognitive processes. Woodcock and Mather (1989, p. 15) define Gv in part: In Horn-Cattell theory, broad visualization requires fluent thinking with stimuli that are visual in the minds eye. They further note that Engineers, auto mechanics, architects, nuclear physicists, sculptors, carpenters, and parts department managers all use Gv to deal with the demands of their job, although many writers seem to consider Gv relatively low-level cognitive ability, more perceptual than intellectual. 17We understand language here in broad sense, as structured system of symbols governed by rules that enable the representation 13 conceptual abstraction, which is the process of creating higher-order categories based on invisible, functional, or relational properties, e.g., understanding that thermometer, clock, and yardstick all belong to the category of measuring instruments, or understanding concepts like justice and entropy. The sort of abstraction tested by visual tasks like ARC and RPM sits somewhere in between, and much closer to the first. It allows us to extract relational structure from perceptual representations, by identifying visual progression (e.g., increases in shape size), rotation or reflection, and patterns like same-colored regions across portions of grid. The corresponding type of reasoning is often called visuospatial relational reasoning or perceptual analogy. While its true that this intermediate level of perceptual abstraction has been historically important in the testing of fluid intelligence, it is conceptual abstraction that is central to science and mathematics and is considered to be the defining cognitive capacity behind theoretical reasoning. As already mentioned, conceptual abstraction involves the formation of general ideas and principles that are detached from any particular sensory inputs. They range from ordinary concepts like cause and effect or kinship (e.g., the notion of an uncle) to scientific quantities like acceleration, the idea of natural selection in biology, random variable in statistics, semigroup in mathematics, and so on. It is these linguistically scaffolded abstractions that allow generalization across widely different contexts and enable sophisticated reasoning, explanation, and prediction. None of these are exercised by visual tests. So, while success on visual tasks might well provide some signal on intelligence, it should not be understood as indicative of general or high-level abstraction ability. The latter goes much farther than what is visually given, by relying on language to represent categories that depend on inferential roles rather than perceptual relations. It might be objected that ARC was never intended to test high-level reasoning or abstraction; what has made it notable is that powerful AI models have found it challenging even though it is fairly simple test that many ordinary people are able to pass. However, ARC aims to be test of abstraction and reasoning simpliciter, not test of perceptual abstraction and visuospatial reasoning. The paper that introduced the benchmark speaks of general measure of intelligence, and the relevant Kaggle competition presents itself as test to find the next frontier of AGI, implying human-level mastery across all cognitive domains. As for the observation that models are struggling with something that even children can do, and the resulting temptation to conclude that models are incapable of reasoning, it should be kept in mind that 5-year-old human has 500-million-year evolutionary head start in visual processing. The human brain dedicates massive portion of its cortex to automatic, unconscious visual grouping (Gestalt principles like proximity, similarity, and closure). For humans, seeing the grid and grouping the pixels requires zero effort. This raises the question of whether the exclusion of language in order to be fair to humans might not tip the scales the other way and make the test unfair to language models. brief historical aside might be helpful here: nonverbal intelligence tests originated in the 1800s because scientists were curious about the cognitive abilities of language-deprived people who could not speak. During World War I, the US Army needed methods to assess abilities of foreign born and illiterate military recruits and committee to design such methods was formed [McCallum 2003][p. 5]. Later in the 20th century, fairness came to be an increasingly prominent motivation behind nonverbal intelligence tests, some of which (such as the Universal Nonverbal Intelligence Test [Bracken and McCallum 1998]) had fairness as their overarching goal [McCallum 2003][p. 88]. After all, if one hardly speaks English, it is not fair to give them an intelligence test that requires fluency in English. By the same token, however, it is not fair to give visual intelligence test to subject who is visually impaired. And to this day, the perceptual abilities of language models, even multi-modal ones, remain relatively poor, lagging far behind their language skills. This is largely because transformer-based models, which were designed for sequence statistics, lack the geometric and physical priors encoded in the human brain, i.e., they lack most of the core-knowledge priors that humans have (objects, actions, and space).19 Given and communication of meaning. The only essential features are the use of discrete symbolic elements, combinatorial principles that determine how these elements can be arranged, and systematic relationships between syntax (form) and semantics. 18Modern developmental science indicates that conceptual abstraction is produced by different learning systems than perceptual abstraction, emerges at different developmental stages, and is subserved by different mechanisms, requiring cross-modal integration, lexicalization, executive function, and the learning of rule-based categories. According to Sloutsky [2010], the brain contains two distinct category-leaning systems. One is similarity-based system characterized by compression, which learns from perceptual correlations, operates on many overlapping features, is present early in development, and is tied to the inferotemporal cortex and perceptual systems; this is the system that learns dense categories like dogs and cats. The other is rule-based system characterized by selection, which selects specific diagnostic dimensions, supports rules, requires attention and executive control, is tied to the prefrontal cortex, and develops later; this is the system that learns sparse categories, like prime numbers and moral agency. Sloutsky [2010] points out that sparse categories tend to depend on unobservable properties and require selective attention and rule learning; and that abstract reasoning in mathematics and science depends on representational resources that go far beyond visual structure: the ability to formulate and manipulate concepts whose defining properties are unobservable, inferred, and linguistically articulated. 19Just as they lack grounding in the physical world, with no direct experience of physical interactions such as movement and object that ARC seems heavily gated by perceptual access to the relevant tasks, visual ability may be acting as confound. This is essentially the argument made by recent work [Wang et al. 2026], which found that models frequently fail on ARC tasks before reasoning even begins, simply because they are unable to reliably identify objects, shapes, and spatial relations (something which is effortless for humans), and therefore end up misinterpreting the visual input. Manual error analysis carried out by the authors suggests that 80% of model failures are due to perceptual errors.
