They also found that when the task is split into 2-stage pipeline, perception stage that takes the image and outputs natural-language description of it, followed by reasoning stage that takes the natural-language description and reasons with it, performance improves significantly when the results of the first (perceptual) step are improved, e.g., simply by using stronger visual model, while keeping the reasoning model fixed. The authors thus argue that ARC results can overestimate the reasoning gap between humans and AI, because humans have much stronger visual object recognition and perceptual grouping. These considerations suggest divergence between the methodological guidelines in the paper that introduced ARC and the empirical benchmark ecosystem that grew around it. Chollet [2019][p. 45] writes that general benchmark of intelligence should work for both humans and machines, fairly, by only assuming the same priors as possessed by humans (e.g. Core Knowledge) and only requiring human-sized amount of practice time or training data and we recommend only comparing the intelligence of systems that assume sufficiently similar set of priors (p. 44). But in practice the test is routinely used to compare the intelligence of humans and transformer-based models, which have radically different priors. To be clear, we are not claiming that all reasoning is linguistic, nor that ARC fails to test reasoning altogether. There are well-established non-linguistic forms of reasoning, visuospatial reasoning being one of them, and ARC does appear to tap abilities in this domain; we would not be surprised if ARC performance is shown to correlate with gV . What remains empirically unclear is exactly which abilities ARC measures and how strongly performance on the benchmark correlates with broader measures of intelligence, and particularly which of the prominent CHC factors it loads on most strongly. Our claim is that vast and very important portion of human reasoning and abstraction is conceptual or theoretical: it concerns categories, relations, and entities that are not directly perceptible and whose representation and manipulation are inextricably linked with language. Because nonverbal tests like ARC exclude language by design and operate over fully observable structures, they leave this large and very significant class of reasoning capabilities untested.20 Finally, even though ARC was designed as test that does not use language, largely in an effort to eliminate reliance on memorized patterns and to measure fluid intelligence, many of the systems that perform best on ARC are neuro-symbolic pipelines that do not perform simple end-to-end prediction of the output grid [Chollet et al. 2025]. Instead, they parse the grid into objects, regions, and colors; they express candidate solutions in DSL (domain-specific language) or restricted Python-like program space; they explicitly search over compositions of primitives, keeping only candidates that fit the training examples; and then run the resulting program on the test input. This process effectively maps these problems back into symbolic language (Python or DSLs) and relies on formal syntax, semantics, and logic to synthesize solution. And given that models are pretrained on terabytes of Python code, this allows them to lean on the massive linguistic and structural priors they memorized during pretraining, which seems at odds with core-knowledge test. While its true that the tasks themselves are purely visual, if the solver is giant model pretrained on vast bodies of Python, iteration patterns, grid manipulation idioms, and so on, and relies on that know-how during problem solving, then language priors re-enter through the back door. 2.1.4 Formal Reasoning The final category comprises benchmarks whose input problems are fully formal, expressed in symbolic logic or formal mathematical notation. These benchmarks are designed to probe reasoning in highly controlled setting, abstracted away from the vagaries of natural language or domain-specific conventions. manipulation, and no experience of temporal continuity. baby learns about object persistence by interacting with the physical world, not by ingesting static Internet snapshot. 20An analogy between reasoning and humor might help to clarify our position. Language is not strictly necessary for reasoning, in the same way that it is not necessary for humorconsider Chaplins silent films, slapstick, timing, visual surprise, incongruous or absurd gesture (Monty Pythons ministry of silly walks), and so on. At the same time, the most effective and intelligent humor is conveyed with the use of language, which enables irony, parody, understatement, narrative framing, double meaning, register shifts, self-reference, and the management of multiple perspectives at once. We would likewise claim that the most sophisticated forms of reasoning are enabled by and expressed through language and symbolism, which allow for explicit premises and conclusions, scope distinctions, quantification, modality, counterfactuals, recursive embeddings, and above all the articulation of why something follows. Taking silent pratfalls as the essence of comedy would be like taking grid transformations as the essence of reasoning. 15 Unlike natural-language or generalist benchmarks, which often involve mix of cognitive competencies and inferential strategies, formal reasoning benchmarks aim to isolate and evaluate deductive capacity in its purest form. The tasks typically involve proving or refuting precise claims, or applying logical rules within constrained formal system. Most benchmarks in the category comprise contentful but formally structured math problems (as in MiniF2F [Zheng et al. 2022]); datasets like PROOFGRID that aim to capture content-agnostic reasoning are relatively rare (Rosetta [Baek, Shaun Lee et al. 2025] is arguably another example, but see our comments below). key advantage of such benchmarks is that they are interpretable and verifiable: because the logic is fully specified, it is possible to determine unambiguously whether response is correct and to assess the structure of the reasoning that led to it. This makes them especially well-suited for fine-grained diagnostic evaluation of models inferential abilities. Moreover, the absence of linguistic ambiguity or commonsense knowledge demands allows these benchmarks to more cleanly separate reasoning skill from linguistic fluency or background knowledge. That said, formal reasoning benchmarks introduce their own complications. They tend to require high degree of familiarity with the underlying logic or proof calculus, which makes them less accessible to general-purpose models or to researchers unfamiliar with the target formalism. Some benchmarks are grounded in complex systems like higher-order logic or dependent type theory, or rely on interactive proof protocols (such as Lean-style tactic traces) that complicate the relationship between proof generation and proof explanation, as we discuss later. PROOFGRID mitigates these challenges by adopting minimalist natural deduction language, featuring the same general style of reasoning commonly found in mathematics and logic textbooks. That language is fully described in concise self-contained prompt. All in all, formal reasoning benchmarks play an essential role in the ecosystem of reasoning evaluation, offering uniquely rigorous lens on deductive competence. 2.2 Reasoning Dimensions To better understand the diversity of reasoning datasets and benchmarks in NLP and AI in general, we position these along three main orthogonal axes: 1. an input representation axis; 2. an output representation axis; and 3. content or crystallized knowledge axis. These axes reflect conceptually independent dimensions that shape the kinds of reasoning that benchmark can evaluate.21 The first axis captures the form in which the inputs of dataset are expressed. This could be naturalistic human language, or synthetically generated natural language, or formal symbolic notation, or something in between. This choice usually reflects the motivation behind the benchmark. Datasets like SNLI and MNLI are squarely in the mainstream NLI tradition and are thus invariably focused on natural language. Their main aim is to test whether language models can simulate human-like reasoning from textual input. Towards the middle of this axis we find datasets like RuleTaker and FLD, which are formulated in the sort of controlled natural language (CNL) described earlier: templated English sentences generated from symbolic rules. This results in data that is expressed in natural language but is formulaic, with very limited linguistic diversity, and semantically aligned with formal logic. Finally, at the right end of this axis we find datasets like Rosetta, whose problems are expressed entirely in formal symbolic notation, using logics of varying representational power (like propositional, first-order, or higher-order logic). The second axis characterizes the structure and complexity of the required model outputs. On one end we have tasks that demand only binary decision, such as whether hypothesis follows from context (e.g., entailment vs. non-entailment), 21The axes are not continuous, but orthogonality is more important than continuity in conceptual framework of this kind. more granular analysis could refine each independent dimension into categorical or ordinal feature with specific set of interpretable values. We have already taken preliminary steps in this direction, e.g., we introduce number of discrete levels for the output-representation axis: binary, multiple-choice, literal, chain-of-thought, entailment tree, Python code, and formal proof. We leave the development of finer-grained and exhaustive classification scheme to future work. Note also that while we treat the input representation and content axes as conceptually orthogonal, in practice they are not always independent. In particular, benchmarks with natural language inputs almost inevitably carry some semantic content, even when synthetically constructed. Although it is possible to design grammatically wellformed yet semantically vacuous sentences in Jabberwocky style (e.g., Every glorb is zibble), such examples are highly contrived. Thus, while the axes are formally distinct, they may be somewhat statistically correlated in practice. 16 or the selection of single correct answer from multiple choices. These setups are straightforward to evaluate and model, but offer limited insight into the actual reasoning that models perform. On the other end of the spectrum are tasks requiring more structured reasoning artifacts, such as chains of deductive steps, natural-language justifications (e.g., COT traces), entailment trees (as in EntailmentBank), or even full formal proofs (as in PROOFGRID). These richer outputs enable finer-grained analysis of models reasoning process and can support interpretability, proof checking, and compositional generalization, but typically demand more supervision and are harder to generate. The third axis characterizes reasoning benchmarks in terms of their content demands, capturing the type and extent of domain knowledge required to solve them. At one end of this spectrum we have content-free benchmarks, such as PROOFGRID, which involve purely symbolic structural reasoning tasks in uninterpreted settings. These tasks rely on the internal structure of the inference tasks and require little external knowledge. Farther along this axis we find benchmarks grounded in commonsense or everyday content, such as SNLI or bAbI [Weston et al. 2016], where understanding is based on familiar types of objects, events, and social conventions. Moving along, we have benchmarks involving scientific content, like GPQA [Rein et al. 2023] or ARC (Challenge Set) [Clark et al. 2018], which require reasoning over concepts and relations introduced by scientific theories, and are thus accessible only to those with some scientific education. At the far end of the axis we have pure math benchmarks such as MATH, GSM8K, or Lean-based proof datasets, where the content is fully mathematical, and reasoning requires both mathematical knowledge and symbolic manipulation. Although diverse, these benchmarks fall along coherent continuum that reflects increasing degrees of semantic specificity, abstraction, and crystallized domain knowledge. Framing this as content axis allows us to compare reasoning tasks not just by how they are formulated, but also by what kind of knowledge they demand. In addition to these three primary axes, we identify three further dimensions that shed light on the internal structure and reasoning demands of benchmark: the expressiveness of the underlying formalism, the range of inferential mechanisms required, and the diversity of its tasks. These axes are secondary and only relevant for datasets that are grounded in formal logic or in natural language rendered from logical forms. Formalism Expressiveness: For benchmarks that are defined in, or translated from, formal logic, including both fully symbolic datasets like PROOFGRID and CNL benchmarks like RuleTaker, key consideration is the expressive power of the underlying logical system. This axis captures the range of propositions that the formalism can represent and thereby constrains the structural and semantic complexity of the problem instances. For example, RuleTaker is grounded in stratified Datalog, which supports recursive Horn clauses and limited negation but disallows full disjunction, unrestricted negation, quantifier alternation, and function symbols. Other benchmarks might be based on propositional logic, first-order logic fragments, equational logic, description logics, epistemic, modal, temporal, or spatial logics, or more powerful systems like higher-order logic or dependent type theory. Each of these formalisms permits different set of logical constructs (such as quantifiers, equality, modal operators, or arithmetic) which in turn determine the space of conceivable problems. Two benchmarks may both use formal representations yet differ dramatically in the depth and nuance of statements they can encode, and thus on the types of reasoning that they can test, depending on where they lie on this dimension. Inferential Repertoire: This axis captures the range and sophistication of inference mechanisms that model must deploy to explain and justify its answers to benchmarks problems. It is mostly relevant for benchmarks that require models to produce structured reasoning artifacts that are amenable to automated analysis, such as entailment trees or formal proofs. For benchmarks that only require final answers, the models reasoning process remains opaque and this axis becomes inert; without observable reasoning traces, it is not possible to determine what inference methods were used, short of speculating about latent cognitive or model-internal dynamics. The same applies to benchmarks that elicit unstructured reasoning artifacts, such as chain-of-thought traces expressed in arbitrary natural language. Even if such traces are generated during inference, they are typically not evaluated and are therefore of little help in analyzing the underlying reasoning mechanisms. When structured reasoning outputs are generated, this axis ranges from benchmarks that require only simple chaining or forward inference using small set of rudimentary rules (such as modus ponens and conjunction elimination) to tasks that demand complex reasoning strategies such as hypothetical proof, arbitrarily nested case analyses, universal generalization, reasoning by contradiction, existential instantiation, and various forms of mathematical induction (natural, structural, strong, etc.). While this dimension often correlates with the expressiveness of the formalism, it is not reducible to it. benchmark may employ an expressive logic in theory while exercising only weak fragment in practice. In such 17 cases, the inferential repertoire axis provides an independent informative lens on the range of reasoning tested by benchmark. Task Diversity: Another important dimension for characterizing reasoning benchmark is task breadththe number and variety of distinct reasoning tasks it includes. Some benchmarks are narrowly focused, posing single core problem, such as entailment: Does hypothesis follow from context ? Even within that core task, variations can arise depending on the output format (e.g., binary label vs. supporting explanation in COT style), but the central reasoning objective remains the same. Other benchmarks expand the task scope by supporting or requiring multiple types of reasoning, each probing different varieties of inferential ability. For example, in addition to entailment verification, benchmark might include tasks like countermodel generation (demonstrating that = h), error diagnosis (identifying why hypothesis fails), or hypothesis repair (suggesting minimal modifications that ensure = h). The number and diversity of these subtasks can be treated as measure of benchmarks functional richness. Greater task diversity broadens what dataset can reveal and enables finer-grained assessment of models reasoning, but it also complicates evaluation and interpretation. Benchmarks with many distinct subtasks can suffer from lack of cohesion, potentially conflating heterogeneous cognitive demands. This can make it difficult to attribute model success or failure to specific competencies. Finally, aggregating performance across many diverse tasks tends to lack principled foundation, raising questions about how to interpret single combined score. As such, while broad task coverage offers diagnostic benefits, there is also value in relatively focused benchmarks with tightly defined reasoning objectives. Diversity is most effective when the subtasks are conceptually coherent and mutually reinforcing, rather than arbitrarily bundled together. This axis thus highlights tension between diagnostic richness and interpretive clarity. Together, these auxiliary axes complement the existing framework and offer finer-grained tools for analyzing what makes benchmark challenging.
