DAG problems are structurally transparent. They feature low fan-in and uniform dependencies, inviting linear forward inference. Nevertheless, even these straightforward problems are tractable only for models with precise multi-hop control. Mid-tier and older models still lose track of dependency order and lemma management, despite the shallow problem structure. 2. Graph-coloring and De Bruijn problems require non-local combinatorial coordination, reasoning over mutual exclusions or cyclic dependencies. 3. The remaining 4 families are the most demanding, requiring reasoning about global constraints, nonconstructive parity, or meta-counting, not just local entailment. We discuss the reasoning challenges posed by some of these problem types in more detail below. The performance plateau observed in the hardest four families could mark the transition between symbolic compositionality, where the best models now largely succeed, and abstract combinatorial reasoning, where inference must coordinate quantitative rather than qualitative relationships, and which appears to remain unsolved. Following the instrumented checkers automatic detection and repair of syntax errors, the remaining model errors can be divided into 4 groups: parsing errors (that could not be auto-repaired); genuine logic/reasoning errors (typically from step whose conclusion does not follow from the listed arguments, or with an argument that does not hold at that point in the proof); reference to an unbound identifier (usually because the identifier is out of scope at that point in the proof, and sometimes because the identifier is hallucinated); 86 Figure 28: PL3-PW distribution of first errors. wrong-conclusion error, whereby the proof (or subproof) does manage to derive some conclusion, but not the expected one. Fig. 28 shows the distribution of these errors. Logical errors dominate overall, especially among the elite models, but there are notable exceptions, e.g., most of the errors made by Grok-4 are parsing errors. However, these are the very first errors (the ones that cause proofs to fail), not all errors made by proof. It is possible, and actually common in our experience, that proof might contain multiple errors of different types, meaning that even if the very first error were somehow repaired or ignored, the proof would still fail later on with different error. For example, manual analysis of the first 30 GPT-5 proofs with parsing errors showed that in most cases the proofs also had subsequent logical errors. For instance, GPT-5s proof for item 25 (a Tseitin-formula problem) fails due to parsing error, but even if that were overlooked, the proof would fail later due to logical error in this step: A8 from (A8 A6), ( A6). So why are subset-cardinality problems challenging for LLMs? After all, these problems encode simple quantitative constraints of the form at least half of these variables must be true or at most half of these variables may be true. However, after translation to propositional logic, all explicit counting disappears. What remains is web of small formulas whose collective effect implicitly enforces global numerical invariants. No single formula, or even any small neighborhood of formulas, is contradictory. Instead, unsatisfiability emerges only when many such formulas are aggregated. Essentially, the solver must rediscover the original counting argument from distributed set of local logical fragments. As an example, consider this bipartite graph: C 1 2 3 4 The left and right subset-cardinality constraints for this graph can be encoded as follows: Left-side constraints: Right-side constraints: {(x1A x1C), (x2A x2B), x3B, (x4B x4C)} {(x1A x2A), (x2B x3B x4B), (x3B x2B x4B), (x4B x2B x3B), (x1C x4C)} This is already an unsatisfiable set of = 9 formulas. (In the PROOFGRID formulation, the task would be to prove that the negation of one of these 9 formulas follows from the other 1 = 8 formulas.) In particular, the left-side constraints force x3B, which yields x2B and x4B by virtue of the right-side constraint (x3B x2B x4B). Resolving these two atoms with the left-side constraints (x2A x2B) and (x4B x4C) gives x2A and x4C. But x2A gives x1A (by the right-side constraint (x1A x2A)), while x4C gives x1C (by the right-side constraint (x1C x4C)). Finally, x1A and x1C contradict the left-side constraint (x1A x1C). It is the requirement to extract latent global arithmetic structure that makes these problems difficult for both LLMs and classical resolution-based SAT solvers. Resolution is inherently local inference mechanism: it combines pairs of clauses by eliminating variables, which is effective for local inconsistency but not very suitable for computing sums, majorities, or other global invariants. Simulating counting via resolution produces wide intermediate clauses, which leads to provably long proofs for such problems. And although LLMs obviously differ radically from SAT solvers in their internal mechanics, they share similar weakness: they are very good at detecting local pattern-based inconsistencies and short causal chains, but they struggle to infer global budget constraints (for example, that the left side of the bipartite graph collectively forces at least variables to be true while the right side collectively limits the total to at most 1). From the models perspective, the necessary contradiction is not nearby in the clause space; it only appears once the entire system is integrated. In short, subset-cardinality benchmarks expose key limitation of present LLM-based reasoning systems: they must perform non-local arithmetic reasoning while operating on representations that offer only highly local symbolic cues. Tseitin-formula problems present similar challenges. Like subset-cardinality instances, they encode global numerical invariant (a parity condition in this case) using only local propositional constraints. In Tseitin encoding, each graph node contributes an equation stating that the XOR of the incident edge variables must equal (be logically equivalent to) node label. Each such constraint is locally satisfiable, and nothing in any single equation suggests inconsistency. The contradiction arises only when all constraints are combined: summing the node equations modulo 2 counts each edge twice and therefore yields an even total, whereas the stipulated node labels sum to an odd total. The system is therefore globally inconsistent even though every local piece of it appears perfectly coherent. It is this global character of the contradiction that makes Tseitin formulas difficult for both classical SAT solvers and for LLMs. In the resolution setting, simulating XOR reasoning inside CNF requires building large intermediate clauses, which leads to the well-known exponential lower bounds for resolution on Tseitin formulas over expander graphs. In other words, resolution has no built-in ability to compute mod-2 sums and must instead simulate parity reasoning indirectly and expensively. Despite their obvious differences, LLMs share an analogous limitation. As already mentioned in connection with subset-cardinality problems, LLMs are good at identifying small, localized inconsistency patterns, but they struggle with algebraic invariants that emerge only after aggregating many constraints. When given Tseitin instance, model sees lot of small formulas with no explicit indication that they collectively impose XOR constraints. To detect unsatisfiability, the model must effectively reconstruct an argument asserting that the parity of all node labels must equal the parity of all edge constraints, global property that is invisible at the level of individual formulas. Thus, Tseitin formulas expose second core weakness of current LLM reasoning abilities: the lack of robust mechanisms for global algebraic aggregation, here in the form of parity rather than counting. Subset-cardinality formulas require discovering hidden integer bounds, while Tseitin formulas require discovering hidden mod-2 invariants. In both cases, the contradiction is not nearby, appearing only after combining many constraints in way that neither resolution nor contemporary LLMs perform efficiently. We close the section with few qualitative observations about the PL3-PW results. Given the simplicity of NDL0s syntax, one might wonder what sort of nontrivial parsing errors models still manage to make. Here is small but representative sample: Hallucinated compositionality, such as compound from steps or formulas with subformulas that are named inline. Heres an example from Claude Opus 4.5 (item 11): step4 := (A8 A13) from premise-49, (A6) from (A6); 88 and one from GPT-5.2 (item 80): block-1 := (((block-1a-formula := ...)) & (block-1b-formula := ...)) from block-1a, block-1b; Natural language text inserted into the proof without comment markers, e.g., here is step from Grok4s proof for problem 10: Wait, in this branch, we are under assm13 = A3, and one from Grok-4s proof for item 83: (c2 ==> false) from that Conditional proofs with empty bodies, e.g. (from Grok-4s proof for problem 22): assume} a3 := A3 { } Atomic inference steps with empty bodies, e.g. (from Claude Opus 4.5 on item 17): disj27 := (A7 A7) from ; Incorrectly formatted comments, e.g., here is proof step by o3-mini (item 41): lemma4 := (A3) from H2, /* contrapositive of (A3 ==> (A2A1)) inferred from premise-1 */; Here o3-mini used C-style comment block instead of hash signs (it did this in 10 proofs overall). Fig. 28 shows that some top-tier models make lot of syntax errorsmore than logic errors. This is especially pronounced for o3, which makes over 3 times as many parsing errors as logic errors. This is odd behavior for top model, since these usually have better cognitive control. closer look reveals that many of these are not genuine parsing failures. They typically occur either because: (a) the model gives up (e.g., o3 explicitly writes in its proof description for item 15: Proof deliberately omitted: problem too complex to resolve within given constraints) and returns an empty proof, which of course fails to parse; or because (b) the model incorrectly concludes that the theorem it was given to prove is not theorem and (falsely) claims to discover counterexample. This also results either in completely empty proof or one that only has comments explaining the alleged counterexample, thus also resulting in parsing error. As an example of (b), GPT-5.2 gives the following proof for problem 12: { } # No proof can be given in NDL for this sequent, because it is not valid. # Countermodel (satisfies all premises, falsifies the target): # # # # Under this assignment, (A3) is false, hence ((A3) ==> (A5 A2 A4 A1)) is true, # and the desired negation ( ((A3) ==> (A5 A2 A4 A1))) is false. A3=true, A1=false, A2=false, A4=false, A5=false, A6=false, A10=false, A8=false, A11=true, A12=false, A13=false, A9=true, A7=false. When the comments are stripped out, this too is an empty proof that doesnt parse. Response patterns (a) and (b) are very different. The former reflects deliberate abstention, where the model explicitly acknowledges its inability to solve problem and refrains from generating an output that looks plausible but is incorrect. If anything, this suggests better calibration and some degree of epistemic awareness. While PL3-PW was not designed to measure abstention, it is nevertheless possible to gauge it indirectly by inspecting proof descriptions and lengths. cursory analysis suggests that o3 is the model that most often realizes its inability to produce working solution and instead returns minimal output consistent with the formatting instructions.78 That said, many syntax errors by other strong models are genuine. This is the case, for instance, for almost all syntax errors by Grok-4 and most by Claude Opus 4.5. Gemini 3.1 stands out for its nearly impeccable syntax control, registering only 3 minor parsing errors across all 300 proofs and zero grammar hallucinations. 78Although here too, as in PL2-PW, o3 often exaggerates the difficulty of these problems. For example, on problem 18 it writes that short proof is impossible due to the extremely intricate web of the 24 premises, adding that fully explicit derivation would 89 It is noteworthy that in over dozen out of the 70 relevant problems, Gemini 3.1 was able to explicitly recognize that the input formulas were encodings of graph-coloring problems, and proceeded to reason about them as such. For instance, here is what the model had to say about problem 144: The provided premises describe graph-coloring problem where each triplet of variables (e.g., A13, A14, A15) represents single node taking one of three colors. We can extract subset of four nodesspecifically the ones corresponding to premise-1 (A13A14A15), premise-2 (A16A17A18), premise-4 (A4A5A6), and premise-5 (A7A8A9)which form complete graph of size 4 (K4). Because each node must take one of exactly 3 colors, and no two connected nodes can share color, these four nodes cannot be successfully colored. This implies the premises are inherently logically inconsistent. We derive this contradiction rigorously through exhaustive case analysis on premise-2, (A16A17A18). For each assumption (A16, A17, and A18), we analyze the options for premise-1 (A13A14A15). In every nested case, the possible colors for the remaining two nodes are restricted, leading to chain of forced implications that systematically rules out all three options for the final triplet, allowing us to derive false. We then use disjunction elimination to bubble up false through all the assumption blocks until we prove false unconditionally from the four premises. This is compelling demonstration that the model has built powerful internal representations that allow it to recover the latent combinatorial structure of an unknown (previously unseen) problem even after it has been compiled away into entirely abstract propositional logic formulas. In this case, Gemini 3.1 managed to infer the underlying graph, reconstruct its coloring constraints, and reason about it at the level of graph theory rather than uninterpreted symbolic logic. That kind of structural reverse-engineering is precisely what makes symbolic reasoning difficult for conventional solvers, yet at times these language models appear capable of performing it robustly. Nevertheless, the contrast with their performance on other problem families (such as subset-cardinality and Tseitin-formula instances) shows that this ability is limited at present. It works well when the hidden structure is relational and pattern-based, but not when it requires detecting global numeric or parity invariants that leave no local syntactic traces. This observation also brings into question the aim of testing fluid reasoning through abstract symbolic problems that are ostensibly free of content. If the task items are drawn from well-known problem families, such as graph coloring, it is evidently possible that strong models will be able to decode them and solve them by relying on crystallized knowledge. Thats fair point. If model can look at compiled symbolic problem instance and infer that it encodes Tseitin formula or pigeonhole structure, then the problem is no longer testing reasoning in vacuum. So PL3-PW should certainly not be viewed as psychometrically pure measure of fluid reasoning (although in any event we do not make that claim for any PROOFGRID tasks). And this does raise questions for future benchmarks. Abstract symbolic formulations of task items can still carry structural fingerprints of their source problem families, and over time models will become better at recognizing such fingerprints and solving problems in schema-conditioned ways. At the same time, this does not necessarily create loophole. De-engineering hidden structure is itself hard reasoning act. loophole would be direct retrieval of memorized proof, superficial matching to canned template, or exploiting accidental formatting cues. By contrast, to successfully decode compiled logical instance back into an underlying combinatorial object, model has to detect subtle regularities; hypothesize latent generating structure; map the symbolic encoding back to that structure; and then solve the problem by exploiting that reconstruction. This is deep and substantive capability, not surface pattern completion. In general, there is an important difference between retrieval of explicit memorized content versus reconstruction of latent structure from the problem instance itself. PL3-PW blocks the first but does not fully block the second, and arguably it shouldnt. If model recognizes the infinity-of-primes proof because it saw it in training, that tells us little. But if model reconstructs an implicit graph from compiled logic formula and then reasons over that graph, that tells us lot. That is not just crystallized knowledge; it is crystallized knowledge being productively brought to bear through demanding inference process. Humans do this all the time (this is really parity argument or this is disguised pigeonhole principle, etc.), and we dont call it cheatingwe call it insight. easily require several hundred carefully checked proof-lines, even though Gemini 3.1 solves the same problem in fewer than 50 steps. This tendency suggests deficits in o3s metacognition that often make it unable to distinguish this is very hard for me from this is objectively very difficult. 90 7.5 Introduction to Epistemic Instability: Remembering Locally and Forgetting Globally We previously discussed, in the context of checking PL1 proofs (p. 3.2), recurring failure of models to exhibit judgment consistency, broad phenomenon that we refer to as epistemic instability. Here we give another illustration, this time more rigorously, in the context of proof writing, which provides an ideal testbed for this kind of analysis. The starting point is theme that emerges upon inspection of incorrect proofs: these proofs often fail due to steps with trivial logical errorsso trivial that one would expect the models that wrote these steps, especially the more sophisticated ones, to be able to easily recognize such steps as invalid. Here is concrete example from the GPT-5.2 proof for PL3-PW problem 96. At some point in that proof, GPT-5.2 writes: A15 from a16-or-a15; where a16-or-a15 denotes the previously derived conclusion (A16 A15). In other words, the model is claiming that A15 follows from (A16 A15). This is an elementary mistake: A15 does not follow from (A16 A15), and in general, does not follow from (p q). Indeed, if we separately ask GPT-5.2 to judge the validity of this inference, it readily gives the right answer along with the obvious counterexample: A16: true A15: false That part is unsurprising, as this is trivial satisfiability question that any model should be able to get right with minimal effort. And GPT-5.2 does get it right, consistently: if we ask it this question multiple times, it will reliably (and quickly) tell us that A15 does not follow from (A16 A15). And yet, in the context of writing proof, the model somehow forgets this elementary fact and claims that A15 does follow from (A16 A15). An example from Gemini 3.1 comes from problem 11, where the model writes: ... A4 from premise-16, A10; ... where premise-16 is (A4 ==> A10). In words, Gemini 3.1 here claims that A4 follows from A4 A10 and A10, which is clearly wrong (its the classic fallacy of affirming the consequent). And again, when we later ask the model to evaluate the validity of this step, it rejects it as incorrect and judges it to be trivial error (on scale of 1 to 5, with 1 being trivial and 5 being very difficult, it rates this inference at 1). It might be that the model confused premise-16 with premise-15, which is A4 A10 and which does imply A4 in conjunction with A10. Similar lexical confusions could be behind Gemini 3.1 errors on steps like A11_1 from ( A11 ==> false) (from item 13); and likewise on false from A11_1, (A11 ==> false) (again from item 13). But fuzzy lexical proximity does not explain errors like false from A6, A7 (item 67), unless one holds that A6 and A7 are easy to confuse; and likewise for steps like A10 from (A8 A10), A8 (item 71) or false from A4, A15 (item 105), or many other similar errors. When we later ask Gemini 3.1 to evaluate the validity of these inferences, it of course recognizes them as wrong and generates the obvious counterexamples. It also rates the complexity of these entailment steps at level 1 or 2 (very simple), with remarks like Its trivial problem. This is strikingly dissimilar to how human mathematicianand indeed any stable rational agentwould operate. competent human writing proofs would not make such elementary logical blunders. beginner who hasnt yet mastered these concepts might make such blunders, but they would typically make them consistently and regardless of the overarching task context, simply because they dont have the level of competence needed to recognize them as blunders. But these giant models do have that level of competence, because when they are given these simple entailment problems, they invariably solve them correctly and demonstrate robust understanding of what does and doesnt follow and why, and they even rate the problems as trivial; and yet somehow they continue to make these errors when writing proofs. To quantify this more precisely, we did the following for each of the top 9 models : we took all proofs written by that had at least one logical error on step of the form from p1, . . . , pn, selected one such erroneous step from each proof, and asked whether follows from p1, . . . , pn. Invariably, all models correctly responded that does not follow 91 Model gemini-2.5-pro gpt-5 gpt-5.1 gemini-3.0-pro-preview claude-opus-4-5-20251101 grok-4 o3 gemini-3.1-pro-preview gpt-5.2 # of Logical Errors Self-Recognized Proportion Mean Self-Rated Recognition Difficulty (1-5 Scale) 161 160 122 114 82 79 61 58 100% 100% 100% 100% 100% 100% 100% 100% 100% 1.67 1.15 1.58 1.26 1. 1.71 1.20 1.52 1.74 Table 15: Proportion of logical errors made by top-tier model when writing PL3-PW proofs that the model itself recognizes as errors, and average rating of the difficulty/subtlety of these errors, given by the same model, with 1 being trivial and 5 being very complex. Here # of Logical Errors is proof-level: one per incorrect proof; we are not counting all erroneous steps. and provided appropriate counterexamples. Table 15 summarizes the results.79 We see that all models were able to flag 100% of their logical errors, and moreover judged those errors to be elementary (with 1 being trivial, 2 easy, 3 average, 4 hard, and 5 very hard, most of these inference errors were rated 1; the mean rating did not reach 2 for any of these models). The results make it clear that proof-generation competence and local-entailment competence come apart in this setting. In Section 8 we sketch the beginnings of quantitative theory of epistemic stability, which we then apply to this data to obtain concrete epistemic-stability scores for the models in Table 15, normalized to the unit interval. In conclusion, the best models fail in this task not because they cannot recognize elementary logical relations, but because that knowledge is not stably preserved under the cognitive load of proof generation; the models know locally but forget globally. In the conclusions, we attribute this partly to failure of computational cognitive modularity. 7.6 PL3-PC This task is similar to PL1-PC, except that it works with NDL0 rather than NDL: given an NDL0 proof for problem, determine if the proof is correct (successfully derives the target conclusion from the premises); and if it is not, specify the first line in the proof that has an error and describe the details of that error. In addition, if the error is in step of the form from p1, . . . , pn and amounts to not following from p1, . . . , pn, the model must produce counterexample, i.e., an interpretation which satisfies every pi but falsifies p. 1, . . . , All PL3-PC problems were solved with ATPs and the resolution proofs were then translated into NDL0. All 300 NDL0 proofs obtained in this way were correct by construction. We postprocessed every proof as follows: for every step from p1, . . . , pn of every proof, we randomly decided whether to (a) inline some argument(s) pi; and/or to (b) append extra arguments k, randomly chosen, for some > 0. Inlining in this context means replacing variable occurrence like lemma-8 by the corresponding formula. This plainly preserves correctness, but it may make verification more difficult since the model must focus more on the formulas themselves rather than their names. Because classical logic is monotonic, adding extra arguments also preserves correctness; these simply serve as distractors. Finally, to avoid severely imbalanced dataset, we corrupted relatively small number of proofs (53), e.g., by negating the conclusion of from step. We used SAT solver to ensure that every corrupted step is indeed incorrect and should therefore receive negative verdict from proof checker. The 300 problems in PL3-PC are largely the same as the 300 problems of PL3-PW, with two main differences: 79As the caption of Table 15 notes, the numbers in the column # of Logical Errors are undercounts because only one error is chosen from each logically incorrect proof. Estimates of the total counts of logical errors across all 300 PL3-PW problems are as follows: Claude Opus 4.5: 1659, Gemini-3.1: 1046, Gemini-2.5-Pro: 925, Gemini 3: 905, GPT-5: 737, Grok-4: 441, o3: 353, GPT-5.1: 340, GPT-5.2: 138. These are conservative estimates that ignore all proof steps with syntax errors, so they might also be undercounts. (In particular, models that make many syntax errors may end up with underestimated logical-error counts because many of their steps are garbled and cannot be analyzed algorithmically; if they were analyzed manually, additional logical errors would likely be discovered, errors that are currently absorbed by syntax noise. This also means, ironically, that models that write cleaner proofs, such as Gemini 3.1, may end up with higher logical-error counts simply because were able to scrutinize more of their proof steps.) Of course, absolute error numbers dont take into account the volume of proof code written by each model. Once we normalize accordingly, we get the following 92 1. The premises of each PL3-PC problem form subset of the premises of the corresponding PL3-PW problem. Thats because in PL3-PC problem we typically retain only those premises that are actually used in that problems proof, whereas PL3-PW problem may have additional, redundant premises. Also, each premise in proof-checking problem may be trivial semantics-preserving transformation of its counterpart in the proofwriting problem, e.g., premise like (A3 A5 A8) might become the contrapositive (A5 A8 A3) in the corresponding proof-checking problem. 2. The De Bruijn problems (items 280 through 294) are larger, starting with the De Bruijn formula Dn for = 11 (instead of = 1, as is the case in PL3-PW) and ending with D39. Because most of the proofs in PL3-PC are flat, having been originally derived from resolution-based ATPs, they test long-context dependency tracking but not hierarchical context management (the latter is tested by PL1-PC and especially by the challenge set PL1-PC-c). Specifically, here are the core capabilities that are stress-tested by PL3-PC: 1. Reference resolution in linearly growing lexical environments: in step like lemma_35 := (A2 & B5 ==> C8) from lemma_5, lemma_2, premise_ can the model reliably retrieve the definitions of lemma_5, lemma_2, and premise_12 from earlier parts of its context, and flag an error if one of them is not found? 2. Logical availability verification (assumption-base tracking), also in monotonically growing logical contexts . Any argument to from may be inlined, e.g., step might be: lemma_9 := (A2 & B5 ==> C8) from lemma_3, (C7 C8 A2), premise_4, B4 So the model must only resolve the names lemma_3 and premise4, but must also make sure that the inline formulas ( C7 C8 A2) and B4 are in the assumption base at that particular point in the proof, i.e., that they are logically available and can be used as ammunition to derive further conclusions. 3. Micro-reasoning under long-context pressure: Can the model verify that follows from p1, . . . , pn (for 5)? The model has to solve dozens or hundreds of these mini-SAT problems in the course of checking single PL3 proof, and it must do so without compromising any of the other subproblems it must solve. This is the main capability tested by PL3-PC (and one that PL1-PC doesnt already test). The main results for PL3-PC are reported in Table 16. The evaluation protocol for this task is lenient in the following sense: true positives (cases where model correctly determines that proof is invalid80) are counted as such when the model ratios: GPT-5: 0.0401, Gemini 2.5: 0.0355, Claude Opus 4.5: 0.0324, o3: 0.0312, Gemini 3.1: 0.0252, Gemini 3: 0.025, GPT-5.1: 0.0204, Grok 4: 0.0139, and GPT-5.2: 0.0092. However, manual inspection of random sample suggests that reports that manage to pinpoint the location of the error also tend to describe it accurately and to generate valid counterexamples, so this leniency is unlikely to affect the results materially. We also list guessing-only accuracies. Based on the accuracies for the full task, the DI (discriminability index) of this task is 0.42, indicating exceptionally high discriminating power. Note that guessing-only mode is of marginal help to strong models. However, as we move down the table and the ability scale, guessing-only mode becomes increasingly beneficial, with accuracy boosts ranging from 2X to 10X for lower-end models. Because verdict that finds the input proof correct does not require supporting evidence, model can easily inflate its score by disproportionately favoring that outcome. This is especially true for an imbalanced dataset like PL3-PC, which only has 53 incorrect proofs out of 300, meaning that trivial policy of always answering correct can already achieve an accuracy of 82%. Verifying incorrect proofs is more demanding, because it requires precise error localization. Accordingly, we also report performance when evaluation is restricted to the set of incorrect proofs, in Table 17. Fig. 30 gives finer-grained picture of proof-checking performance, indexed by problem type. It shows that most of these problem families are easy for top-tier models, with De Bruijn proofs being the most notable exception. As we have already pointed out, these are considerably longer than the other proofs, and they also feature very large inlined formulas that are hard to process without reliable algorithm. However, mid-tier and third-tier models exhibit good deal of performance variation across these problem families. For example, dag-pyramid problems are almost universally accessible (e.g., Llama-405b by Nous Research and Mistral Magistral reach 70% and 40% there, respectively), despite being the longest proofs in the dataset (apart from De Bruijn proofs). By contrast, proofs from the relativized-pigeonhole problem family, for example, are much more challenging for such models (e.g., the accuracies of Llama-405b and Mistral Magistral fall to 21% and 3% there, respectively). We use the same error taxonomy we used for PL1-PC, with the exception of type-5 errors, which are not relevant in this setting: 1. Type-1 errors: The model incorrectly claims that the proof is right (false negatives). 2. Type-2 errors: The model incorrectly claims that the proof is wrong (false positives). 3. Type-3 errors: Formatting issues (e.g., responses missing expected fields). 4. Type-4 errors: The model correctly claims that the proof is wrong but fails to localize the error. Model Accuracy Guessing Only Model Accuracy Guessing Only gemini-3.0-pro-preview 0.91 ( 0.08) 0.98 ( 0.05) gemini-3.1-pro-preview 0.88 ( 0.09) 1.0 ( 0.03) gpt-5.1 0.83 ( 0.1) 0.98 ( 0.05) claude-opus-4-5-20251101 0.75 ( 0.11) 0.91 ( 0.08) gemini-2.5-pro 0.67 ( 0.12) 0.87 ( 0.09) gpt-5.2 0.6 ( 0.13) 0.64 ( 0.13) gpt-5 0.64 ( 0.13) 0.91 ( 0.08) 0.6 ( 0.13) 0.81 ( 0.1) 0.52 ( 0.13) 0.75 ( 0.11) claude-sonnet-4.5-ext-thinking 0.49 ( 0.13) 0.76 ( 0.11) deepseek-r1-0528 0.42 ( 0.13) 0.62 ( 0.13) o3-mini o4-mini 0.26 ( 0.12) 0.6 ( 0.13) deepseek-v3.1 deepseek-reasoner 0.17 ( 0.1) 0.46 ( 0.13) hermes-4 0.3 ( 0.12) 0.89 ( 0.09) 0.21 ( 0.11) 0.85 ( 0.1) 0.17 ( 0.1) 0.75 ( 0.11) claude-sonnet-4-20250514 0.08 ( 0.08) 0.91 ( 0.08) claude-opus-40.04 ( 0.06) 0.92 ( 0.08) grok-3 gpt-4o 0.02 ( 0.05) 1.0 ( 0.03) mistral-magistral 0.02 ( 0.05) 0.94 ( 0.07) llama-3.1-405b deepseek-v3 0.02 ( 0.05) 1.0 ( 0.03) gpt-4.1 0.02 ( 0.05) 0.87 ( 0.09) 0.02 ( 0.05) 0.58 ( 0.13) 0.02 ( 0.05) 1.0 ( 0.03) Table 17: PL3-PC accuracies and guessing-only accuracies restricted to the set of incorrect proofs. 95 grok-4 Figure 30: PL3-PC accuracy per problem type. We see that type-2 errors dominate across all models and ability levels. Deepseek-V3, in particular, makes stunning 247/247 type-2 errorsa false-positive error rate of 100%. The model invariably hallucinates non-existent errors. Type-1 errors, where buggy proof is wrongly pronounced correct, are much less common, with Deepseek-Reasoner taking the lead with 28 such errors (out total possible of 53, for an error rate of 53%), followed by Llama 405b and o4-mini (41% and 40%, respectively). Gemini 3.1 is the only elite model that did not make even single type-1 error, while the two top Gemini models also made very few type-4 errors, testament to their strong debugging skills, and specifically their ability to reliably identify subtle errors in complex reasoning chains. GPT-5.2 underperforms on this task, with false-positive rate of 12%. Figure 31: Error distribution across the 4 error types for PL3-PC. 96 Figure 32: PL3-PC accuracy, excluding De Bruijn problems, as function of proof length for the top 12 models. Fig. 32 plots accuracy as function of proof length for the top 12 models. We exclude De Bruijn problems because those 15 proofs are significantly longer than the others and there are not enough of them for robust plot. The figure shows that proof size is not strong degrader of PL3-PC performance, at least in this particular evaluation regime of flat NDL0 proofs with no more than few hundred steps, which translates to inputs with considerably fewer than 10K tokens.81 Modern attention mechanisms show robust retrieval for context windows of that size (and even for substantially larger ones). In conclusion, even though few models already attain very high absolute accuracy on PL3-PC , the task remains sharply discriminative overall and exposes substantial qualitative differences in error calibration, localization, and robustness. Moreover, the observed ceiling effects are contingent on conservative corruption and scoring choices (only 53 problems were noised, in straightforward way), and could be pushed back simply by increasing corruption density or requiring stronger justifications. (As the results show, accuracy drops for all models when evaluation is restricted to the set of incorrect proofs.) Accordingly, PL3-PC is not quite solved at present and should instead be viewed as scalable diagnostic test of verifier-side semantic reasoning (based on entailment rather than inference rules).

sections.title: 8 A Quantitative Measure of Epistemic Stability
sections.content: When rational agent α asserts proposition, they make themselves accountable for what others can infer from it. These inferences may be made by pure deduction, but more often they are made by commonplace inferential practices in the shared space of norms that govern the use of the relevant concepts. We might say that, by asserting p, α becomes committed to additional propositions q1, q2, . . . that represent epistemic obligations, meaning that α cannot deny any qi on pain of epistemic incoherence. One of the qi is itself, and in that case the epistemic obligation reduces to familiar selfconsistency: If α asserts p, then α cannot also deny (or assert p).82 But there are many other epistemic commitments. If we claim = Paris is the capital of France, we commit ourselves, among other things, to the propositions that France is sovereign country and that Paris is city in France. Unless we willfully aimed to be uncooperative communicators, it would be incoherent to deny these facts while maintaining p. Let be any problem that may be meaningfully posed to model . We distinguish between the general problem and particular instances of P.83 We assume there is prompt associated with and we write π(P) for the core part of 81Excluding De Bruijn problems, the average number of BPE tokens in PL3-PC input, counting both the detailed instructions and the input proof, is 6.9K (computed with tiktoken using the cl100k_base encoding). The median is 6.6K tokens. With De Bruijn proofs, these become 7.7K and 6.6K, respectively. 82Non-monotonic reasoning, of course, is commonplacepeople often change their minds. But typically they do so upon receiving new information, at which point they might retract p. They do not maintain both and at the same time. 83The general problem can be understood as the overall specification of the task (Given the name of country, respond with its 97 that prompt, namely, the input-insensitive part that gives the general specification of P. Even if two problems and involve the same task, they may be formulated in incompatible ways. For instance, π(P) and π(P ) may assume different epistemic postures or operate within different epistemic contexts, e.g., π(P) may be aimed towards truth or factual coherence whereas π(P ) may be sarcastic or adversarial, or both may be aimed towards truth but π(P) may be operating within the context of Euclidean geometry whereas π(P ) may adopt hyperbolic geometry. We say that and are compatible iff no presupposition or instruction of π(P) contradicts one of π(P ), and neither prompt provides new information to that is not also provided by the other.84 This is symmetric relation. We write (x, y) to mean that given as input, model has generated as the output; and we refer to (x, y) as judgment j. Now let and be two compatible problems and let and be two instances of and , respectively. We write (x, y) (x, y) and say that (x, y) epistemically constrains (x, y) iff generation of as the answer to creates an epistemic obligation for to generate as the answer to x, because not doing so would be epistemically incoherent. We call this positive epistemic obligation. Suppose poses geography questions under truthful system prompt π(P), instance asks for the capital of France, and has generated the expected answer, Paris, as the output y. Suppose further that asks multiple-choice trivia questions, also under truthful system prompt, and that asks whether Paris is city in France, Portugal, or Venezuela. Then would be normatively bound to generate France as the answer y. We take as primitive relation for present purposes. negative epistemic obligation, written as (x, y) (x, y), obtains iff generation of as the answer to precludes it from generating as the answer to x. Typically, positive epistemic obligation creates number of trivial negative epistemic obligations, e.g., if generates Paris as the answer to question above, then it is negatively bound from generating Portugal or Venezuela or any other country as the answer to x. But the converse may not be true: negative constraint from to need not impose positive epistemic constraints on . For instance, let and be the proof-checking and proof-writing problems, respectively. If claims that proof is incorrect, it is negatively bound from generating as the answer to the corresponding instance of , but this does not impose any positive obligations on , as is free to generate other proofs for the relevant problem. In the converse direction, however, we have positive epistemic obligations: If generates as the answer to an instance of , then is epistemically obliged to answer yes to the instance of that asks whether is correct deduction.85 Now fix an empirical distribution D1 of observed input-output pairs {(x1, y1), . . . , (xm, ym)}, where each xi is an instance of and (xi, yi); and corresponding distribution D2 = {(x is an instance of compatible problem and (x j). It is notationally convenient to view D1 as distribution of judgments (x1, y1), . . . , (xm, ym), and likewise for D2. Define the set IncD1[D2] as follows: n)} where each 1), . . . , (x n, 1, j, IncD1[D2] = {j D2 D1 . }. (13) In words, this set contains all judgments in D2 that are precluded (negatively constrained) by, or are incompatible with, some judgment in D1. We can quantify the degree of epistemic stability relative to these two distributions simply by computing the size of this set, dividing it by (the size of D2), and subtracting the result from 1. This gives us measure of epistemic stability wrt to D2 and D1, written as ESID1 [D2]. We drop the subscripts D1, D2 when they are immaterial or obvious from the context. We refer to this quantity, 1 (IncD1[D2]/n), as the epistemic stability index of relative to D2 and D1. Thus, ESI ranges smoothly from 0 to 1, with higher values obviously being preferable. The ratio IncD1[D2]/n has natural probabilistic interpretation, as the empirical probability that randomly chosen judgment from D2 is incompatible with some judgment in D1. Accordingly, 1 minus this ratio is the probability of the complementary event, namely, that randomly chosen judgment from D2 is compatible with all judgments in D1. We want this number to be as close to 1 as possible. We augment this intuition with soft log-based transformation that adds curvature reflecting nonlinear penalties for judgment inconsistencies, as well as diminishing returns from such penalties: ESID1 [D2] = 1 (cid:20) 1 + γ log (cid:21) IncD1[D2] D2 log(cid:2)1 + γ(cid:3) (14) population and area in square kilometers.), while an instance of the problem is specific input to the task (France). 84By new information we mean information that did not appear in pretraining corpus. 85Modulo answer variation due to stochasticity. We assume here that all judgments are robust, i.e. (x, y) means that is stable 98 Figure 33: ESI curves for different values of the curvature parameter (γ). The x-axis is the fraction IncD1[D2]/n. We still have ESI = 1 when IncD1 [D2] = 0 (perfect epistemic stability) and ESI = 0 when IncD1[D2] = D2 (complete incoherence), but the penalty for relatively small IncD1[D2] grows faster than in the linear case, accentuating even small amounts of instability, while saturating smoothly towards 0 as IncD1 [D2]/D2 1. The parameter γ controls the curvature and can be tuned depending on how stringent we want to be; we use γ = 5 as reasonable default. Figure 33 shows different ESI curves for γ {2, 4, 6, 10}. We assume that D2 is not chosen adversarially, with prior knowledge of the constraints in D1. Otherwise it would be possible to cherry-pick data so as to compute any desired ESI value. To avoid metric gaming, the selection of D2 must be independent of D1, and in particular must not occur after inspecting the judgments in D1. Methodologically, this requirement is somewhat analogous to the familiar principle that test data must not be tuned to models performance on the training set. While this independence condition can be made fully precise (for example, by fixing the generation process for D2 in advance, or by requiring D2 to be sampled from distribution specified independently of D1), we do not need to formalize it here. For present purposes, it suffices to note that allowing D2 to be selected in response to observed judgments in D1 would undermine the intended interpretation of this stability measure. As concrete application of ESI, consider the results of Section 7.5 and let be any of the models in Table 15. We may take D2 to be the set of 300 proofs written by in response to the PL3-PW prompt, or more precisely, the set (x, y) where is an input prompt from the PL3-PW dataset and is the corresponding output proof generated by . Now consider the set of all individual steps from p1, . . . , pn that appear in any of those 300 proofs written by (S will have thousands of elements), and let D1 be the set of all entailment/SAT judgments made by for each of those steps. In other words, D1 consists of all pairs (x, y), one for every individual step from p1, . . . , pn in S, where is an input prompt asking whether follows from p1, . . . , pn, along perhaps with an estimate of the difficulty of making that judgment, and is response. We can then use the data in Table 15 to compute ESID1[D2]. The number of self-recognized errors becomes the size of the set IncD1[D2], i.e., the set of all -written proofs that are precluded by some -made entailment judgment in D1. Plugging in the numbers from Table 15 into the defining formula (14) yields the values shown in Table 18. The results show that all top-tier models suffer from severe epistemic instability. Even for the most stable model, GPT-5.2, there is nearly 40% chance that randomly chosen PL3-PW proof written by it will be inconsistent with its own relevant entailment judgments. More than half of these elite models have ESI values less than 0.5, meaning that any proof written by such model is more likely than not to disagree with the models corresponding entailment judgments. mode of conditional distribution over outputs given x. Note, however, that in mathematical tasks, persistent answer variation when given one and the same problem instance is itself sign of weak reasoning, indicating that the model doesnt have stable internal representation of the solution space. 99 Model gemini-2.5-pro gpt-5.1 claude-opus-4-5o3 gpt-5.2 ESI Model gpt-5 gemini-3.0-pro-preview grokgemini-3.1-pro-preview 0.27 0.38 0.48 0.53 0. ESI 0.27 0.41 0.52 0.61 Table 18: Epistemic stability values for PL3-PW proofs relative to individual entailment judgments for the 9 models of Table 15. models ESI value can be understood as the probability that randomly chosen PL3-PW proof written by the model is consistent with the models individual entailment judgments. Ideally, all of these values would be 1. At its core, ESI is fraction, and therefore it is inherently asymmetric. Disregarding the log transformation, it is essentially the probability that random draw from D2 is not precluded by any draw from D1. We are thus normalizing by D2, not by anything symmetric across both distributions. This is to be expected, because ESID1[D2] and ESID2 [D1] measure different qualitative phenomena; each has different unit of analysis (the objects in D2 and D1, respectively). For instance, with D1 and D2 as defined in this example, ESID1[D2] is proof-level score, whereby proof in D2 is incompatible with D1 iff it contains at least one step that diverges in D1. So in this case the ESI metric serves as an all-steps-must-pass aggregator. If we flip D1 and D2, then ESID2[D1] would become step-level score that would address very different question: what fraction of the models entailment judgments survive contact with the corresponding proofs? Both quantities may be of interest, but no matter what D1 and D2 are, any low values for ESID2[D1] are source of concern. In general, epistemic stability is not an absolute scalar property. It is defined with respect to reference distribution, for which there is no universally correct choice. Definition (14) is general, but genuinely different stability concepts can be singled out by instantiating the distributions in different ways. For example, above we took D2 to be the set of all 300 PL3-PW outputs by , but we could instead consider the subset comprising only the incorrect outputs generated by (while leaving D1 unchanged). Doing so would pick different measure of epistemic stability, one that we might call error-triggered ESI, or ESI ET for short. (We do not report ESI ET values here for PL3-PW, but it suffices to note that they are very low, e.g., 0.14 for Gemini 3.1 and 0.38 for GPT-5.2.) The first version of ESI can be viewed as global or generic measure of epistemic stability, while the second is conditioned on errors. The difference between the two is roughly analogous to the difference between blood pressure at rest vs blood pressure under stress. They measure two different aspects of epistemic stability. An agent with high error-triggered stability makes errors that are part of stable internal theory, even if that theory is wrong. In other words, the agent may be wrong but they are wrong in unified and stable way. This is common for humans (disregarding trivial performance errors). By contrast, low error-triggered stability means that the agents mistakes are not stably believed, arising instead from breakdowns in deployment, control, or integration. This appears to be common for language models, which often have the competence to make the right local judgments (such as micro-SAT entailments) but fail to recruit that competence during more complex and global tasks (such as proof writing). It may be noted that epistemic stability is not unequivocally desirable. In particular, the capacity for self-correction and epistemic stability appear to pull in opposite directions. Low stability values indicate poor integration of different types of competence, but they also suggest that the model possesses latent knowledge that can be recruited by external scaffolding or iterative self-checking and verification. High stability values, by contrast, indicate that the models mistakes are part of stable internal reasoning pattern and are thus less amenable to agentic self-correction. So there seems to be tradeoff: low epistemic stability is bad for unified reasoning but good for self-aided corrigibility, while high epistemic stability is good for unified and internally stable reasoning but not for self-repair. While this tension is generally real, in practice epistemic stability is the more valuable primitive; we need systems that are stable, self-consistent, and predictable across contexts. At any rate, the tension is only with self-correction, not with correction in general. From an engineering perspective, one does not need single model to, e.g., both generate and verify. Corrections can be made by external verifiers or critic agents rather than by relying on the same model to repair its own inconsistencies.

sections.title: 9 PL4
sections.content: 9.1 Proof Writing in Hilbert Systems This division contains 250 proof-writing problems in the Hilbert calculus for propositional logic. Because writing proofs in Hilbert system is challenging, we have split this division into two tasks: 100 1. the main PL4-PW task, consisting of 200 problems that are shorter and easier on average than PL1-PW items; 2. challenge set, PL4-PW-c, consisting of 50 problems randomly sampled from longer PL1-PW problems. The Hilbert calculus is flat proof system that does not admit conditional subproofs: proof is just list of formulas, each of which is an instance of an axiom schema or else follows from previous steps by single inference rule, modus ponens. This flat linear structure makes Hilbert systems primarily of metatheoretical interest (e.g., for studying soundness and completeness), because the lack of assumption scope and the pared-down rule set make it easier to prove theorems about these systems than within them. But for benchmarking purposes, these same factors make Hilbert proofs substantially harder to write than proofs in natural deduction. Formulas here are defined exactly as in NDL, built up inductively from propositional atoms and logical connectives. In the variant we use, which is the most common formulation of the Hilbert calculus, the only two primitive connectives are negation and implication. Disjunctions, conditionals, and biconditionals are desugared using only these two connectives. In particular, is written as q, is written (p q), and as ((p q) (q p)). Conditional elimination (modus ponens, also known as detachment) is the only inference rule; it derives from and p. In addition, any proof step may infer any instance of an axiom schema (a logical formula template). For example, Axiom 1 of the calculus is (p (q p)), where and are metavariables ranging over arbitrary formulas. Any formula that matches this template is an axiom, such as (A (B A)) or (A ((B C) A)). The standard version of the Hilbert calculus that we use formalizes classical logic of implication and negation with only three propositional axiom schemas:86 Axiom 1: (p (q p)) Axiom 2: (p (q r)) ((p q) (p r)) Axiom 3: (p q) ((p q) p) derivation of target tautology is sequence of formulas p1, . . . , pn = p, where each pi is an axiom instance or else follows by modus ponens from two earlier formulas. This format is sufficient in principle, but, for convenience, we name each line and provide justification either by citing an axiom or by listing the arguments to modus ponens. For example, the following is proof produced by o4-mini for the tautology ((A A) A). Each axiom is implemented as primitive method in Athena [Athena Language Team 2025, p. 103], which matches the input sentence to the respective axiom schema or reports that no match is possible. In this way the proof becomes fully verifiable using Athena. { } p0 := axiom-1 on (A ==> ((A ==> A) ==> A)); p1 := axiom-2 on ((A ==> ((A ==> A) ==> A)) ==> ((A ==> (A ==> A)) ==> (A ==> A))) p2 := ((A ==> (A ==> A)) ==> (A ==> A)) BY mp on p1, p0; p3 := axiom-1 on (A ==> (A ==> A)); p4 := (A ==> A) BY mp on p2, p3; p5 := axiom-3 on ((A ==> A) ==> ((A ==> A) ==> A)); p6 := ((A ==> A) ==> A) BY mp on p5, p4 We also permit the use of premises, treating them essentially as axioms. As result, we can pose problems of the form prove that follows from {p1, . . . , pn} in addition to pure-tautology problems of the form prove p1 pn p. This is an important change that allows models to perform suppositional reasoning and emulate natural deduction, yielding flexible knob controlling problem difficulty. As we explain later, it also enables some interesting experiments gauging the ability of models to simplify proof construction in this system by intelligently exploiting the famous deduction theorem [Mendelson 2010][p. 28].87 86The first two axiom schemas are known as and S, respectively, as they correspond precisely to the and combinators of combinatory logic [Hindley et al. 1972], namely λ . λ . and λ . λ . λ . (f x) (g x). To see the correspondence of the latter with Axiom 2, read as function of type (q r), as function of type q, and as variable of type p. We then we have (f x): and (g x): q, so that (f x) (g x) is of type r, corresponding to the tail occurrence of in Axiom 2. Dropping Axiom 3 and keeping only and gives rise to the classical logic of minimal implication. 87The deduction theorem, independently discovered by Tarski [1930] and Herbrand [1930], states that if there is Hilbert proof of from premises {p1, . . . , pn, pn+1}, then there is Hilbert proof of (pn+1 p) from {p1, . . . , pn}. 101 Figure 34: Problem size distributions for PL4-PW (left) and PL4-PW-c (right). PL4-PW, the (easy) 200-problem task, was constructed as follows: (a) 20 problems were hand-picked simple tautologies that appear as standard textbook exercises, such as (A (A B)), (A A), and (A A); and (b) 180 randomly selected problems from the same distribution from which PL1-PW problems were sampled. We targeted short problems (mean length 46 characters) to keep the task feasible. Any problem using logical connectives other than negation and implication was converted to an equivalent formula using only negation and implication, as described above. Only 15 out of the 200 problems are pure tautologies; the rest have at least one premise. The size distributions for problems in PL4-PW and PL4-PW-c, measured as the combined AST sizes of the original premises and goals, are shown in Figure 34. Table 19 summarizes the mean and max numbers of atoms, negations, conjunctions, disjunctions, conditionals, and biconditionals in these datasets. The average problem size in PL4-PW is about 21, compared to about 28 for PL1-PW. In the challenge dataset PL4-PW-c, the average problem size is 40. 9.2 PL4-PW Results We evaluated model performance under two settings: (1) strict setting in which the model is required to produce proof that can be transformed into an Athena-verifiable proof without any post-processing; and (2) lenient setting in which we apply the following limited programmatic corrections to superficial syntax issues and certain formatting errors in applications of modus ponens: We use parsing algorithm that can repair certain formulas with unbalanced parentheses by adding or removing parentheses at the right end of the formula to restore balance; this recovers some formulas that would otherwise be rejected by the Athena parser. Formula Feature Mean Max 52 Size 19 Atoms 23 Negations 0 Conjunctions 0 Disjunctions 15 Conditionals 0 Biconditionals 20.97 8.24 7.17 0.00 0.00 5.55 0. Table 19: PL4-PW Problem Statistics. Size = total number of nodes across all formulas; Atoms = number of propositional variables; Negations, Conjunctions, Disjunctions, Conditionals, Biconditionals = counts of respective logical connectives. 102 We allow different parenthesization styles, including minimal parenthesization where connective application is inferred according to Athenas precedence and associativity rules. We repair missing or superfluous semicolons and other simple syntax errors that are readily fixable by programmatic search-and-replace. We correct flipped argument order in applications of detachment when the swap yields valid rule application. The above can be seen as limited amount of proof-checking instrumentation (compared to the more extensive interventions in NDL). PL4-PW results for both modes are shown in Table 20. Gemini 3.1 is in class of its own in this task, far ahead of all other models. Top accuracies in both modes are shown in bold. The DI for PL4-PW is 0.13 (for lenient-mode results). The lower DI is due to the overall lower performance. For the challenge set PL4-PW-c, we only evaluated selection of elite models. The results are shown in Table 21. On this task, Gemini 3.1 solved 6 out of 50 problems, GPT-5.2 solved 3, Gemini 3 solved 1, and all other models failed to produce single correct proof. Evaluating additional weaker models on this task would not be meaningful. Table 21: PL4-PW-c accuracies for top-performing models. In general, writing proofs in Hilbert calculus is challenging task that requires high ingenuity and exceptional skill and precision in symbolic manipulation and pattern matching. This is borne out by the poor overall performance of current models even on very simple tautologies. Table 22 shows the number of steps in successful proof for each model and formula, for 5 of the simplest problems in the dataset. dash indicates that the model failed to produce correct proof. Gemini 3.1 was the only model to solve all 5 problems. 9.2.1 Error Analysis Errors in this task are grouped into the following categories: 103 Model gemini-3.1-pro-preview gpt-5.2 gemini-3.0-pro-preview grok-4 claude-opus-4-5-20251101 deepseek-reasoner gpt-5 gpt-5.1 gemini-2.5-pro o3 Number of Proof Steps φ1 φ2 φ3 φ4 φ5 9 25 - - - - - - - - 25 25 25 - 25 29 - 89 - - 19 19 19 - 19 19 19 19 - 55 - - - - - - - - - 59 - - - - - - - - - φ1 : (A A) φ2 : (A (A B)) φ3 : ((A B) (B A)) φ4 : ((A (B C)) ((A B) C)) φ5 : ((A (B C)) (C (B A))) Table 22: Performance of leading models on 5 simple tautologies in PL4-PW. The number of steps of successful proof is shown for each model and formula; dash indicates failure to produce correct proof. Failed MP: The model applied detachment incorrectly. Failed Axiom: The model used an axiom improperly. These errors are essentially axiom hallucinations, where the model falsely claims that sentence is an instance of an axiom. Undefined/Hallucinated Intermediate Step name: This occurs when intermediate-step name pi is quoted in an application of modus ponens without being introduced in previous step. Wrong Conclusion: The proof reaches conclusion other than the goal. No Proof : The model did not generate proof. This occurs in two ways: either the model fails to generate any response after multiple API calls for given problem, or the model returns response that does not contain proof. (This is especially common for o3, occurring in 102 out of the 200 problems.) Syntax Error: The model returned proof with syntax error that our automated repairs could not fix. Figure 35: The distribution of error types in PL4-PW under lenient proof checking, for the first error that caused proof failure (or the failure to return proof). 104 The distribution of these errors in PL4-PW (under lenient proof checking) is shown in Figure 35. Incorrect applications of modus ponens in PL4-PW fall into one of three types: 1. the first argument is not conditional formula (a type error of sorts); 2. the rule is applied to two formulas and where = (also type error); and 3. at least one of the two arguments is not in the assumption base (a strictly logical error). Most failed-mp errors made by the strongest models are of type 2. 9.2.2 Proof Length Analysis Success rates in this task correlate strongly both with problem size and with the length of the generated proofs. Figure 36 shows model accuracies as function of problem size, defined as the total AST size of the premises and goal. Performance degrades rapidly as problem size increases, even though problems in this task are quite shorter than those in PL1-PW. Successful proofs for this task are generally short. The shortest proofs for the vast majority of solved problems have fewer than 30 lines. Statistics on the lengths of all proofs written by all models for PL4-PW, similar to those in tasks like PL2-PW, are shown in Figure 37. For each model , the figure shows the distribution of length over the set of all proofs written by (blue), as well as the distribution of length over the set of all correct proofs written by (green). Again, for most models the green distributions (correct proofs only) are narrower and shifted left compared to the blue distributions (all proofs), again showing that correct proofs tend to be shorter. However, some strong models (gpt-5.2, gpt-5.1, gpt-5, and even grok-4, deepseek-reasoner, and o3) generate correct proofs of higher mean length than that of all their proofs. Several models write extremely long incorrect proofs (Gemini 2.5 produced the longest, proof with 2045 lines), suggesting that these models may generate long, verbose proof attempts when they are unable to solve problem. We also compared set of strong models in terms of the length of their successful proofs, by calculating the average proof length across the problems that all of these models managed to solve (under lenient settings): gpt-5.2, grok-4, gpt-5.1, gemini-3.0-pro-preview, deepseek-reasoner, gemini-2.5-pro, claude-opus-4-5-20251101, and claude-sonnet-4.5-ext-thinking.
