Note that neither gemini-3.1-pro-preview nor gpt-5.2 is included because they did not have at least 3 incorrect proofs among these problems, and number of weaker models are not included because they did not have at least 3 correct proofs among these problems. As general trend, most models tend to write correct proofs that are similar in length to the shortest correct proof, with the exception of gpt-5.1 and grok-4, which tend to write longer correct proofs. Among incorrect proofs, some models tend to write much shorter proofs. Other models tend to write much longer proofs, with gemini-3.0-pro-preview having the largest ratios; it could be said that these models tend to persist in proof writing even when they know that the proof is not succeeding. As first noted in connection with Table 22, we observed that models usually return proofs of identical length for the same problem. This is often an indication that the models have converged on the same proof. To further investigate this, we computed the pairwise similarity between model proofs as follows. First, we defined the fingerprint of proof as the counts of each axiom and applications of detachment in the proof. According to this measure, two proofs are identical iff they have the same length, the same counts of each axiom application, and the same number of detachment applications. Second, we defined the sequence of proof as the order in which the axioms and detachment applications appear in the proof. According to this measure, the proofs must have identical fingerprints and, additionally, the same precise order of application of the axioms and detachment. Figure 38 shows pairwise similarity heatmaps according to the fingerprint and sequence measures for PL4-PW. For each pair of models, fingerprint similarity is the percentage of commonly solved problems for which the two successful proofs have the same fingerprint (counts of each axiom and detachment applications), while sequence similarity additionally requires the same order of axiom and detachment applications. We include model in the heatmap only if it shares at least 10 commonly solved problems with every other included model (models are ordered by their performance for inclusion, although this ordering does not affect the final inclusion list). As the heatmaps show, the models demonstrate remarkable degree of convergence to essentially the same proofs. Proofs that match in fingerprint are often identical up to some reordering of the applications of different axioms and MP. The results of this section show that even when the underlying problems are unchanged from PL1-PW (and are, in fact, drawn from the easier end of that distribution), changing the proof formalism to one that does not mirror human reasoning increases the level of proof-writing difficulty by an order of magnitude. PL4-PW thus stands apart from other propositional-logic tasks in PROOFGRID, all of which are rooted in natural deduction. Writing Hilbert proofs does not test human-style reasoning. Instead, it probes the ability to compile proofs into very sparse calculus that is essentially machine language for deduction. It places much higher pressure on symbolic manipulation discipline than on high-level semantic reasoning about the problem. As such, PL4 is complementary to the other PROOFGRID divisions, providing compelling additional axis: proof-writing skill under deliberately unforgiving and syntactically brittle formalism. 107 However, there is an interesting direction for future work that would go beyond raw symbolic muscle to test human-like meta-reasoning: study whether models are able to exploit the deduction theorem in Hilbert systems, instead of attempting every proof directly from the 3 axioms. For example, to prove implication transitivity, (A B) (B C) (A C), human would typically not search for pure (premise-free) Hilbert derivation from first principles. Instead, they would first derive from the temporary assumptions A, B, and C, and then repeatedly apply the deduction theorem to discharge these assumptions and obtain pure Hilbert proof of the conditionalized tautology. Since this discharge process is constructive (and can be carried out by simple algorithm), it suggests natural controlled experiment: compare model performance on the premise-based Hilbert proofs in PL4-PW with their performance on the corresponding conditionalized (premise-free) versions. This would address the question of whether model can solve conditionalized Hilbert problem by first solving the easier premise-based version and then mechanically compiling the solution into pure Hilbert proof. model that can operationalize the deduction theorem in roughly the same way human reasoner would is expected to degrade only moderately between the two settings, whereas sharp drop would suggest that it is not leveraging natural meta-level proof compilation strategy. Although we do not pursue this experiment here, it points to promising way in which Hilbert systems may probe not just low-level proof synthesis but also strategic reasoning about proofs. 9.3 Equational Logic Equational reasoning sits at the heart of mathematics, and by extension, of science and engineering. Work in all of these fields routinely involves equations between apparently different expressions that refer to the same entity. Engineers use equational reasoning to show that two circuit designs are functionally identical, physicists to track conserved quantities, and compiler engineers to verify that back-end optimizations yield identical program semantics. In computer science, the use of rewrite rules to reason about equations constitutes Turing-complete model of computation, forming the foundation of functional programming and playing key role in program verification. Any model aspiring to master these domains must internalize equational reasoning as core competency. Equational reasoning offers particularly attractive testing ground for AI models because it is almost entirely languageindependent. Proofs do not need to rely on the specialized syntactic rules of any particular formal system, but can instead be written as simple sequences of steps where each step is just term annotated with the equational axioms used to derive it from the preceding term.88 Such representations are both rigorous and machine-checkable, while avoiding the burden of ensuring adherence to the idiosyncrasies of formal language. Despite this structural simplicity, equational reasoning is remarkably rich and powerful, capable of encoding subtle and sophisticated arguments. Indeed, in the general case equational reasoning is undecidable, which underscores that the expressive reach of this style of reasoning is more than adequate for probing the limits of AI models. Reasoning about chains of identities requires both domain-independent syntactic skill (carrying out manipulations correctly) and domain-specific semantic insight (grasping the meaning of equivalence in particular theory). In this paper we focus on the former. Using an uninterpreted signature, we introduce three tasks that evaluate models general ability to recognize and apply identity transformations. Because complex terms are inherently structured as recursive syntax trees, these tasks also probe models ability to process hierarchically organized data with rigor and precision. Specifically, we introduce EQ1, dataset containing 1K purely equational (unconditional) proofs. Each example can be viewed as pair that includes two main components: set of equational axioms {E1, . . . , Em}, where, for easy reference, all equational axioms are given unique names: E1, . . . , Em; and derivation of an identity = from those axioms. The structure of derivations is described below. Each axiom is of the form x1, . . . , xn . = r, where the xi are variables and l, are standard first-order terms built from variables, constants, and function symbols of nonzero arity, e.g., g(a, (Y )). Variable names start with upper-case letters, while function symbols and constants start with lower-case letters. We use a, b, c, and as constants. Unary function symbols are , f1, . . .; binary symbols are g, g1, g2, . . .; ternary symbols are h, h1, . . .; and quaternary function symbols (of arity 4) are r, r1, . . .. We dont use any function symbols with arity greater than 4. 88Of course, the terms must still be written in formal notation, such as plus(succ(X), times(a, zero)). But this requires nothing more than the standard convention of function symbol followed by parentheses enclosing comma-separated arguments. This notation is both universal and simple, and is already extensively represented in the generic corpora on which language models are pretrained. 108 If op is function symbol of arity n, the abstract syntactic structure of term of the form op(t1, . . . , tn) is captured by tree with the function symbol op at the root and with the trees corresponding to t1, . . . , tn as its children, ordered from left to right. Constants and variables always appear at the leaves. Viewing terms as trees in this way, positions within term are represented using Dewey notation. Thus, if = g(f (a), g(X, f2(Y ))), position [] corresponds to the subterm of at the root (i.e., itself), position [1] corresponds to (a), [1, 1] corresponds to the constant (leaf) a, [2, 1] corresponds to X, [2, 2] to f2(Y ), and so on. For term and valid position in t, sub(t, p) denotes the subterm of at p. We write Dom(t) for the set of all valid positions in t. substitution θ is function from variables to terms, e.g., θ = {X (cid:55) (a, ), (cid:55) b}. We write θ(t) to denote the result of applying θ to term t, namely, the term obtained from by replacing every variable in the domain of θ with the term θ(v). Thus, for the given example, θ(g(Y , g(X, Z))) = g(Y , g(f (a, ), b)). We say that term matches term s, or that is an instance of s, iff there exists substitution θ such that = θ(s), in which case we say that matches under θ (or wrt θ). proof of an identity = from set of equational axioms is sequence of > 0 steps of the following form: = s0 = s1 = s2 ... ... = sn by by ... by where sn is syntactically identical to t. Such proof can be understood as chain of identities leading from to t. Each step is justified by citing one or more of the axioms in E, listed after the by keyword. The following is sample equational proof of 5 steps that derives the identity g(h(c, a, c), h(a, c, a)) = g(g1(g4(f1(f5(f3(a))), d), e), g2(a, c)) from set of 6 equational axioms. (Universal quantifiers are omitted when listing the equational axioms; all variables are assumed to be universally quantified.) - Axioms: E1: h(c,V1170,c) = h(V1170,a,f4(f1(c))) E2: h(V1173,V1174,V1173) = g2(V1173,V1174) E3: h(a,V1181,f4(f1(c))) = g1(f2(f4(V1181)),f4(e)) E4: f2(f4(V1184)) = g(f3(V1184),f2(e)) E5: g(V1187,f2(e)) = g4(f1(f5(V1187)),d) E6: f4(V1188) = V1188 - Proof: = g(h(c,a,c),h(a,c,a)) g(h(a,a,f4(f1(c))),h(a,c,a)) g(h(a,a,f4(f1(c))),g2(a,c)) g(g1(f2(f4(a)),f4(e)),g2(a,c)) g(g1(g(f3(a),f2(e)),f4(e)),g2(a,c)) g(g1(g4(f1(f5(f3(a))),d),e),g2(a,c)) by E1 by E2 by E3 by E4 by E5, E6 # step 0 # step 1 # step 3 # step 3 # step 4 # step 5 proof is correct (or sound) iff for every step si = si+1 by Ei1, . . . , Eik , the identity si = si+1 follows logically from Ei1 , . . . , Eik . By Birkhoffs seminal result [Birkhoff, Garrett 1935], this amounts to the identity si = si+1 being derivable from the cited equations using the inference rules of equational logic.89 more algorithmic characterization of this derivability relation is based on the notion of rewriting. Given an equation of the form = r, we write to mean that has subterm at some position such that sub(s, p) matches wrt to substitution θ and is identical to except that sub(t, p) = θ(r). In words, is obtained from by replacing the subterm sub(s, p) that matches the left-hand side of under θ with the corresponding instance of the right-hand side r, i.e., θ(r). We refer to sub(s, p) as redex for (or an e-redex) and to sub(t, p) = θ(r) as the corresponding contractum. If t, we say that rewrites or reduces to in one step. For set of equations E, we write to mean that for some E. We write and for the symmetric closures of and , respectively, and for the reflexive, symmetric, and transitive closure of . As Birkhoff first noted [Birkhoff, Garrett 1935], = is provable from set of equations using the inference rules of equational logic (and thus, by Birkhoffs completeness theorem, = is logical consequence of S) iff t. In our case, proof step si = si+1 by Ei1 , . . . , Eik is correct iff the identity si = si+1 follows from the set of equations = {Ei1, . . . , Eik }, which is to say, iff si si+1; and proof as whole is correct iff every such step is correct. 89There are various options for which equational inference rules to take as primitive, but standard choice consists of reflexivity, symmetry, transitivity, and congruence (also known as substitution of equals). 109 The above definition of equational proofs does not impose any requirements on the granularity of the individual steps. This has the advantage of allowing for equational proofs with widely different levels of detail, depending on ones preferences (e.g., depending on the intended audience). On the flip side, it allows for proofs that are very hardpotentially impossibleto check by algorithm. In the extreme case, one could have correct proof of very complicated identity = with just one inference step = s0 = by that cites all given axioms. To rectify this and to simplify the equational tasks considerably, we stipulate that in step si = si+1 by Ei1, . . . , Eik , the term si+1 must be obtainable from si by one-step rewrites using the cited equations, and moreover, that the relevant redexes occur in mutually disjoint positions of si, and thus, in principle, all rewrites could take place concurrently in one single step. (Two positions are disjoint iff neither is prefix of the other.) All proofs in EQ1 adhere to this structure and have extremely simple steps that cite only one or at most two equations, which are only used for one-step reductions in the sense just described. More precisely, if = {E1, ..., Ek} is set of > 0 equations, we write =S iff contains distinct redexes, one for each Ei S, in disjoint positions; and is obtained from by replacing each of these redexes by the corresponding contractum. By definition, if =S t, then does not have any redundant equations; each of the equations in is used to reduce unique redex in s. The reducibility relation =S is proper subset of the usual ; the latter is implied by the former but not conversely. Consider the proof given in the preceding example and take the first step, s0 = s1: g(h(c, a, c), h(a, c, a)) = g(h(a, a, f4(f1(c))), h(a, c, a)) by E1. The underlined redex h(c, a, c) of s0 matches the left-hand side of E1 under the substitution θ = {V1170 (cid:55) a}, and s1 is obtained from s0 by replacing this redex by the corresponding instance of E1s right-hand side, namely the contractum θ(h(V1170, a, f4(f1(c)))) = h(a, a, f4(f1(c))). The entire proof with underlined subterms, using red for redexes and blue for contractums, is shown below: = g(h(c, a, c), h(a, c, a)) = g(h(a, a, f4(f1(c))), h(a, c, a)) = g(h(a, a, f4(f1(c))), g2(a, c)) = g(g1(f2(f4(a)), f4(e)), g2(a, c)) = g(g1(g(f3(a), f2(e)), f4(e)), g2(a, c)) = g(g1(g4(f1(f5(f3(a))), d), e), g2(a, c)) by E1 by E2 by E3 by E4 by E5, E6 Here, only the penultimate term contains multiple (two) redexes; all other steps follow by replacing single redex by single contractum. The two redexes occur in disjoint positions, as is always the case when more than one equation is cited. We started with 1K randomly generated examples, where each example included its own set of equational axioms and proof. All 1K proofs were correct by construction. To balance the dataset with incorrect examples, we randomly corrupted about half of the proofs by introducing errors of two types: 1. Incorrect contractums: On proof step from si to si+1, we randomly corrupted contractum in si+1 so that si+1 is no longer derivable from si by the equations cited by the step. 2. Incorrect equations: We randomly changed one of the justifying equations for given proof step. Thus, e.g., while the originaland correctversion of the step cited equations E2 and E5, we might change that to E9 and E5, ensuring that the step no longer goes through. Only one error was inserted in each corrupted proof, at randomly chosen step. We introduce and study three EQ1 tasks that fall along spectrum of increasing difficulty, forming natural progression that probes different reasoning skills: 1. Proof checking (EQ-PC, easiest): This is largely local and low-level matching and substitution task, although we will see that even here there are different gradations of difficulty depending on how much explanatory detail we demand. 2. Equation recovery (EQ-ER, intermediate): This requires more search and matching than simple checking, but its still constrained to single step at time. 110 3. Gap filling (EQ-GF, hardest): This is synthesis task. The model is given start and end point separated by multiple elided steps and must generate valid intermediate path. This requires multi-step lookahead, global planning, and search through combinatorial space of possible rewrites. Its considerably more complex than the other two tasks. 9.3.1 Equational Proof Checking This task contains 200 randomly chosen examples from the set of 1K equational proofs. The slice is balanced so that about half of the chosen proofs are correct, while the others are corrupted as described above. The model is asked to determine whether the proof is correct, and more importantly, to justify its answer. Accordingly, the requested output has two components: binary correctness verdict and an explanation/justification for the verdict. Checking an equational proof in detail means verifying that each correct step goes through owing to the cited identities, and not only identifying incorrect steps but pinpointing the exact source of error. In particular, if model claims that proof with steps is correct, then its full explanation must be sequence of structured objects, one for each proof step si = si+1 by Ei1, . . . , Eik for = 0, . . . , 1, elaborating why each step is correct. The structured object consists of the relevant proof step index (the number + 1) along with list of rewrites corresponding to the redexes in si. Each rewrite is dictionary with the following keys: redex: the redex in question; position: the exact position of the redex in si; equation: the identity whose left-hand side matches the redex; contractum: the corresponding contractum in si+1. More concretely, the models output must be dictionary with two keys, correct and explanation. The value of correct is true or false, depending on whether the model determines that the input proof is correct. When correct is true, the value of explanation must be list of dictionaries, each with two keys, step (the step number, one for each step of the proof) and rewrites, list of rewrites of the form described above. Thus, for the example in the preceding section, an appropriate output would be: {"correct": true, "explanation": [{"step": 1, "rewrites": [{"redex": "h(c,a,c)", "position": [1], "equation": "E1", "contractum": "h(a,a,f4(f1(c)))"}]}, {"step": 2, "rewrites": [{"redex": "h(a,c,a)", "position": [2], "equation": "E2", "contractum": "g2(a,c)"}]}, {"step": 3, "rewrites": [{"redex": "h(a,a,f4(f1(c)))", "position": [1], "equation": "E3", "contractum": "g1(f2(f4(a)),f4(e))"}]}, {"step": 4, "rewrites": [{"redex": "f2(f4(a))", "position": [1,1], "equation": "E4", "contractum": "g(f3(a),f2(e))"}]}, {"step": 5, "rewrites": [{"redex": "g(f3(a),f2(e))", "position": [1,1], "equation": "E5", "contractum": "g4(f1(f5(f3(a))),d)"}, {"redex": "f4(e)", "position": [1,2], "equation": "E6", "contractum": "e"}]}]} By contrast, if correct is false, then the value of complete explanation must be single dictionary corresponding to the first incorrect step in the proof, and should be: 1. either of the form {"step": ..., "equation": ..., "position": ..., "expectedContractum": ..., "actualContractum": ...} indicating that the contractum for the equation and redex in question (at the given position) is not what was expected by the cited equation; 111 2. or of the form {"step": ..., "givenEquations": [...], "correctEquations:[...]}, indicating that the cited identities do not succeed in justifying that step, though the identities listed under correctEquations do. Outputs of the first form capture errors introduced by corrupting contractums; outputs of the second form capture errors introduced by noising cited identities. The correct contractum for this redex is f2(g(b, f4(c))), but the one that actually appears in the last term is f2(g(b, f4(e))). Accordingly, an appropriate output for this proof is: {"correct": false, "explanation": {"step": 3, "equation": "E5", "position": [3, 1], "expectedContractum": "f2(g(b,f4(c)))", "actualContractum": "f2(g(b,f4(e)))"}} Finally, here is an example illustrating the second type of error, the citing of incorrect equations: - Axioms: E1: E2: E3: E4: E5: E6: E7: g(V1068,d) = f(f2(f3(V1068))) g(b,V1071) = h(V1071,f4(a),d) g(b,V1072) = f5(V1072) f2(V1073) = f1(V1073) f3(V1080) = V1080 f4(V1083) = g2(V1083,f5(b)) f(V1086) = f1(V1086) - Proof: = h(g(b,d),g(b,b),g(b,e)) h(f(f2(f3(b))),g(b,b),g(b,e)) h(f(f1(f3(b))),f5(b),h(e,f4(a),d)) h(f(f1(b)),f5(b),h(e,f4(a),d)) h(f(f1(b)),f5(b),h(e,g2(a,f5(b)),d)) h(f1(f1(d)),f5(b),h(e,g2(a,f5(b)),d)) by E1 by E2, E3, E4 by E5 by E2 by E7 # step 0 # step 1 # step 2 # step 3 # step 4 # step 5 The error here is on step 4: E2 does not justify the passage from h(f (f1(b)), f5(b), h(e, f4(a), d)) to h(f (f1(b)), f5(b), h(e, g2(a, f5(b)), d)). 112 There are no redexes in h(f (f1(b)), f5(b), h(e, f4(a), d)) that match the left-hand size of E2. However, the step is justified by E6, which rewrites the redex f4(a) to g2(a, f5(b)). Thus, the correct output in this case is: {"correct": false, "explanation": {"step": 4, "givenEquations": ["E2"], "correctEquations": ["E6"]}} Our few-shot prompt for this task contains several ICL examples of both correct and incorrect proofs, along with appropriate corresponding outputs. What was described above is task that requires thorough and detailed checking of equational proofs. We refer to this as level-3 proof checking. This is what our proof-checking prompt for equational logic specifies. Nevertheless, to paint richer performance picture, we distinguish two additional evaluation modes that require less detail. In total, we have: Level 1: The model is given full credit as long as it can guess whether the proof is correct, i.e., as long as it makes the right binary judgment. Accuracy at this level measures models ability to get the vibe of the proof. Is it roughly correct or does something seem off? Level 2: If the model rightly claims that the proof is correct, then it must provide detailed evidence for its claim, i.e., the explanation it outputs in that case must be fully correct. This level makes no demands on the models output for incorrect proofs. As long as the model correctly guesses that the proof has an error, it is given full credit even if it gets the error details wrong. Thus, level-2 accuracy measures models ability to perform the computational work of positive verification: identifying redexes, matching them to equations, and confirming the contractums. Level 3: As already mentioned, level-3 proof checking is the most thorough: credit is given only if the verdict is correct and the models explanation gets all the details right, both for correct and for incorrect proofs. Thus, this level requires everything required by the previous levels, as well as debugging skills, or the ability to perform error analysis by precisely identifying and explaining the first point of failure in an incorrect proof. This is the highest level of proof-checking scrutiny. The model must keep track of the expected state (what the term should look like after the rewrite); compare it against the actual state; and then articulate the discrepancy. This level also requires degree of error-repair ability, insofar as the model must single out the correct (or expected) contractum in the case of contractum corruption, or the correct equations in the case of an equation corruption. These levels form ladder of proof-checking rigor: proof-checking success at L3 (level 3) entails L2 success, which entails L1 success, but the converses do not hold. Results for all three levels are shown in Table 26. For L1, the random-guessing accuracy baseline is already 50%. Thus, above-chance accuracy for the top-performing model, Gemini 3.1, is 98%-50% = 48%. If we normalize, we can say that Geminis accuracy is (0.98 0.5)/(1 0.5) = 0.96, i.e., that Gemini 3.1 is achieving 96% of the possible improvement over random guessing. This is near-perfect performance @L1. However, beyond the top 13 models, performance drops to below 70% even at L1. So top-tier and some mid-tier models have very strong intuition that can reliably distinguish correct equational proofs from broken ones, but the rest have lot of room for improvement. The worst-performing models do about as well as random guessing. 113 Performance across the board degrades sharply when we move to L2 and require models to not simply guess correctness but explain on step-by-step basis why the proof is correct, namely, which are the relevant redexes matching the cited equations, where exactly they are located in the preceding steps term, and what are the corresponding contractums. Opus 4.5 experiences 25-absolute-point drop in going from L1 to L2, while for Gemini 3 and GPT-5.2 the drop is 22 and 15 absolute points, respectively. This shows that even for elite models, there is large difference between simply guessing that proof is correct and properly articulating its correctness in structured explanation. Gemini 3.1 is the only top model whose decline is relatively contained (5 points only). Performance drops further at L3, which extends the detailed-explanation requirement to incorrect proofs. At this level, model must do everything it must do for L2 but also document where and how an incorrect proof fails. For some top models (especially for Gemini 3.1 and Gemini 3), the L2 L3 drop is basically zero, and considerably smaller than their L1 L2 drops. This suggests that these models are competent debuggers, almost as good at diagnosing errors as they are at verifying correctness. By contrast, most mid-tier models (like DeepSeek-R1-0528, o4-mini, and o3-mini), experience catastrophic L2 L3 drops. These models are much better at verifying correct stretch of equational reasoning than diagnosing flawed one. Taking the L3 numbers as the main set of results, the DI of this task is an exceptionally high 0.33. The L2 error distribution is shown in Fig. 39. We distinguish 6 error types at this level. Recall that, at L2, if model declares proof correct then its explanation must include list of rewrites for every proof step, where every rewrite is dictionary with four keys: position, redex, contractum, and equation. Error types 36 correspond precisely to one of these 4 values being wrong. 1. Type 1: Ill-formatted responses, such as JSON-schema violation (e.g., response dictionary that does not include one of the expected keys) or an explanation for proof that is declared correct but is missing some of the proof steps.90 2. Type 2: Wrong correctness verdict. 3. Type 3: Wrong position in rewrite of an explanation step. 4. Type 4: Wrong redex in rewrite of an explanation step. 5. Type 5: Wrong contractum in rewrite of an explanation step. 6. Type 6: Wrong equation in rewrite of an explanation step. Error types 1 and 2 are mutually exclusive with types 36, but the latter can co-occur in an explanation (and even on single explanation step), so Fig. 39 reports raw error counts summed over all proof steps. For that reason, the absolute values of these counts cannot be meaningfully compared across models with widely different accuracies on the set of correct proofs. For example, claude-sonnet-420250514 makes far fewer errors than gemini-2.5-pro, but thats because it only recognizes 27 out of the 103 correct proofs, whereas gemini-2.5-pro correctly recognizes 91, so naturally it will have more opportunities to make errors of types 36. The plot shows that misidentifying redex positions (type-3 errors) is the most common failure mode at L2. Models usually output positions that are close to the correct position but do not actually match it. We quantify this closeness relation by defining similarity metric that considers how much of the path is shared between the correct position and the model-generated position before the two diverge, i.e., their longest common prefix. The more steps they share from the root, the more similar they are. The metric is normalized so the score always lies between 0 and 1. If the two positions are identical, the score is 1; if they diverge immediately at the root, the score is 0. For intermediate cases, it strikes balance between the amount of common structure and the total lengths of the paths. For instance, [3, 3, 1] and [3, 3] share prefix of length 2 and one is prefix of the other, so their similarity is high (0.8), while paths that diverge early get much lower scores. The mean similarities for all models across their type-3 errors are as follows: gemini-3.1-pro-preview: 0.83, gpt-5.2: 0.77, gpt-5.1: 0.75, gpt-5: 0.76, gemini-3.0-pro-preview: 0.75, o3: 0.71, o4-mini: 0.70, claude-sonnet-4-20250514: 0.69, claude-opus-4-5-20251101: 0.68, grok-4: 0.66, gemini-2.5-pro: 0.66, deepseek-r1-0528: 0.65, deepseek-reasoner: 0.65, claude-sonnet-4.5-ext-thinking: 0.65, 90We are lenient in these judgments, e.g., instead of checking for strict equality between the set of expected dictionary keys and the set of generated keys, we check only for subset relation, as some models unilaterally insert extra key-value entries, such as note that provides additional commentary about something. 114 Figure 39: Level-2 error distribution for equational proof checking. grok-3: 0.62, claude-opus-4-20250514: 0.61, o3-mini: 0.60, deepseek-v3.1: 0.53, gpt-4o: 0.5, gpt-4.1: 0.5, llama-3.1-405b: 0.45, hermes-4: 0.41, mistral-magistral: 0.38. DeepSeek-V3 makes no type-3 errors. These numbers track only modestly with overall performance, featuring some notable exceptions such as claude-sonnet-4-20250514, which is better at locating precise positions than both gemini-2.5-pro and grok-4, suggesting that this might be fairly distinct skill, not just corollary of general reasoning ability. The results indicate that most models are fairly good at what and why but bad at where. They can often correctly identify the string being changed (redex), what it changes into (contractum), and even the rule that governs the change (equation); but they fail at precisely locating that change within the larger tree structure. This should not be surprising, given that models do not explicitly build or traverse syntax trees. Tree structure is inherently two-dimensional, so transformer-based model must infer it from syntactic cues in the 1D token sequence that it actually sees, primarily from parentheses and commas, which tends to be fuzzy and imprecise for the purposes of exact hierarchical localization. GPT-5.2 stands out here as having the strongest localization skills. As mentioned earlier, raw counts of type-3 errors cannot be compared across models that accept widely different numbers of correct proofs. However, for models that have similar acceptance rates on correct proofs, comparing the counts of error types 36 can reveal strikingly different failure regimes. Focusing specifically on type-3 errors, we see that GPT-5.2 accepts 98 out of the 103 correct proofs and makes total of only 8 type-3 errors. By contrast, Gemini 3 Pro Preview accepts 102 out of the 103 correct proofs and makes 175 such errors. Normalizing, we get type-3 error rate or 0.08 for GPT-5.2 and 1.7 for Gemini 3, difference of about 1.32 orders of magnitude. Even Gemini 3.1, the top-performing model, makes 26 type-3 errors, for an error rate of 0.25, about 3 times larger than GPT-5.2. How well models identify the precise positions of subterms of interest, such as redexes, is likely indicative of their general ability to navigate deeply nested tree-structured data. 115 Among exclusively L3 errors, the most common type for most models is misidentifying the step where the proof first goes wrong. Further, from the two types of possible proof errors, one due to contractum corruption and one due to corruption of the justifying equations, models have harder time pinpointing the details of the former. They struggle roughly equally in identifying the expected contractum and the actual contractum. The discrepancy makes sense. An equation corruption is an easier diagnosis: see change from to v. The cited rule is Ei, but this rule doesnt apply at all. contractum corruption, by contrast, is much harder: see change from to v. The cited rule is Ei. The redex matches the LHS of Ei, so the rule does apply. Now, must compute the expected contractum and compare it to the actual v. This requires the model to perform forward inference step itself, hold the result in memory, and then perform comparison, so its more computationally demanding. 9.3.2 Recovery of Elided Equations This task also contains 200 samples drawn from the set of 1K examples, evenly split between correct and incorrect proofs. The input given to the model is the same as in the previous task, set of named equations and proof D, except that all justifying equations are now omitted from every step in and the model has to discover them on its own. Specifically, for every step of the proof that derives term si+1 from term si, the model has to output set Si of equations that justify the step in the sense that si =Si si+1, assuming that such set Si exists. If it does not, e.g., because the step from si to si+1 has an error, then the correct output is the empty set of equations. For example, given the input: - Equations: E1: h(c,V1170,c) = h(V1170,a,f4(f1(c))) E2: h(V1173,V1174,V1173) = g2(V1173,V1174) E3: h(a,V1181,f4(f1(c))) = g1(f2(f4(V1181)),f4(e)) E4: f2(f4(V1184)) = g(f3(V1184),f2(e)) E5: g(V1187,f2(e)) = g4(f1(f5(V1187)),d) E6: f4(V1188) = V1188 - Proof: = g(h(c,a,c),h(a,c,a)) g(h(a,a,f4(f1(c))),h(a,c,a)) g(h(a,a,f4(f1(c))),g2(a,c)) g(g1(f2(f4(a)),f4(e)),g2(a,c)) g(g1(g(f3(a),f2(e)),f4(e)),g2(a,c)) g(g1(g4(f1(f5(f3(a))),d),e),g2(a,c)) # step 0 # step 1 # step 2 # step 3 # step 4 # step 5 the following list would be correct output: [{"step": 1, "supportingEquations": ["E1"]}, {"step": 2, "supportingEquations": ["E2"]}, {"step": 3, "supportingEquations": ["E3"]}, {"step": 4, "supportingEquations": ["E4"]}, {"step": 5, "supportingEquations": ["E5", "E6"]}] Note that this task has no unique correct output for given step from si to si+1, as there may be multiple sets of equations such that si =S si+1 (although typically the number of possibilities is small). We thus verify each answer independently. If an answer is non-empty set of equations, we check that si =S si+1. If an answer is the empty set of equations, we check that there is no such that si =S si+1. This is easily decidable, first by filtering those candidate equations that match some subterm of si, and then checking whether every disagreement between si and si+1 can be resolved by one of these candidates.91 This task has strong abductive-verification flavor. For every step of the proof, the model is faced with three pieces of evidence: the starting state si, the ending state si+1, and the equational axioms in as the permissible physical laws. The claim to be investigated is that si+1 is easily derivable from si by set of equations (where easy derivability means no deduction chains, just single-step reductions as defined by =S ). To decide the claim, the model must work backward from the evidence by looking at the effect (the difference between si and si+1) and trying to find cause (a minimal set of axioms) that would explain it. This can be performed by difference analysis followed by hypothesis generation. Difference analysis aims to identify the subterm(s) that changed in the transition from si to si+1; this is the 91The set of disagreements between two terms and consists of those minimal positions Dom(s) Dom(t) such that the subterms sub(s, p) and sub(t, p) have different roots. By minimal we mean that if and is strict suffix of then D. Thus, this set is simply the set of earliest or highest positions where the two trees have mismatch. 116 locus of the analysis. Each subterm change consists of redex candidate in si and transformed version in si+1, for which the model generates hypotheses: Could axiom E1 explain this transformation? Could E2? Could E3? While the task requires processing good deal of highly structured data, it lends itself to pattern recognition and does not require fine-grained analysis. In the running example, for instance, the first step can only be justified by E1, since thats the only equation whose right-hand side can introduce subterm of the form f4(f1(c)). Likewise, the second step is easily seen to follow by E2. We report two metrics on this task. The main one is proof-level accuracy (or PLA for short): the fraction of proofs for which the model generates correct outputs at every step. The secondary metric is step-level accuracy (SLA): the proportion of individual proof steps for which the model produces correct outputs. The former is clearly more demanding, as model is rewarded only if it handles correctly every step of the proof. The step-level metric is much more forgiving, as it rewards partial success on individual steps. Proof-level and step-level accuracies are shown in Table 27. The SLA metric gauges raw ER capability. Top models have basically perfect internal mechanisms for solving this problem. PLA, by contrast, measures reliability and consistency. Here only two models manage to score higher than 80%: GPT-5.2 and GPT-5.1, with the former getting nearly perfect score. The rest of the models, even elite models like Gemini 3, are not reliable enough to get every step right consistently across an entire multi-step proof. single mistake in 20-step proof is enough to fail the PLA metric. This shows that moving from high capability to high reliability in sequential reasoning tasks remains critical frontier for most LLMs. PLAs DI (discriminability index) is exceptionally high at 0.36. 9.3.3 Error Analysis We partition the set of all possible step-level errors into 4 types: 1. The models output is ill-formed. 2. The model gives the empty list of supporting equations for step from si to si+1, indicating that the step cannot be =S -justified by any E, when in fact it can. 3. The model gives non-empty list of supporting equations for step when in fact the step cannot be =S - justified by any E. 4. The model gives non-empty list of supporting equations for step and the step is justifiable, but the particular equations given in do not justify the step. proof-level error is this: Every element of the list given by the model is properly formatted, but instead of containing an element for every step of the proof, the list is cut off prematurely or only contains answers for some of the proof steps, but not all of them. We refer to this as type-5 error. Step-level rates for the 4 possible error types are shown in Figure 40. Note that the SLAs of Table 27 are not equal to 1 minus these error rates. That is because the error rates of Fig. 40 are normalized with respect to the total number of steps that the model has attempted to explain (these are the values n= shown above each bar in the figure). By contrast, the 117 Figure 40: Normalized distribution of step-level ER errors. SLAs of Table 27 are normalized with respect to the total number of all steps across all proofs, regardless of whether the model has provided response for step. As an example, consider gpt-4o and llama-3.1-405b. These models only provided answers for small fraction of all steps, particularly gpt-4o, which only handled 200 out of total of 3904 steps, mere 5%. In fact, only 10 models engaged with all possible steps; the rest omitted at least few. The number of steps omitted (the difference between 3904 and the value of shown in the figure) is direct absolute measure of models type-5 errors. No model makes formatting error. The large majority of ER errors are of type 4. Models correctly identify that the step is valid but they provide the wrong set of justifying equations. This is not failure of pattern recognition in the simple sense. The model is usually in the right ballpark but lacks precision. It suggests failure of meticulous verification. Models seem to rely on heuristics like Axiom E5 looks very similar to the change see and then confidently output E5 without performing the rigorous computational step of applying the substitution and ensuring that it produces exactly the target term. Type-2 errors (where the model misclassifies the step as unjustifiable by outputting []) are different failure mode that cannot be explained simply by lower reasoning competence, since there are several models with poor performance but no type-2 errors. The rate of type-2 errors likely depends on whether models post-training alignment prioritizes helpfulness and engagement versus abstention in the presence of uncertainty. Competence still determines how often the model is uncertain, but whether the model tries to engage despite the uncertainty is largely shaped by its alignment regimen. Another finding involves bidirectional rewriting. Our formalization explicitly defines =S as left-to-right only, which suffices for this dataset. Yet several models repeatedly choose sets of equations that can only justify step if reduction is performed from right to left. Strictly speaking, these answers are incorrect because they do not adhere to the prompt instructions, which only allow for left-to-right rewriting. We give full credit to such answers anyway, because they are logically correct, even if not consistent with the prompt. While this happens relative rarely,92 it is curious that model would choose equations that require right-to-left rewriting when equally simple equations that work from left to right (and are thus fully consistent with the prompt) are available. This suggests that many models are following the spirit of equational reasoning (proving two things are the same) while violating the letter of the specific task instructions (only rewriting from left to right). In other words, their deeply ingrained semantic understanding of equality as symmetric 92o3-mini leads the pack, with bidirectional rewrites in 32 proofs (out of the total 200); followed by GPT-4.1 (17 proofs). Many others use bidirectional rewriting more sporadically. 118 relation is overriding the specific procedural constraints of the task. This underscores how deeply learned priors from pretraining (such as the symmetry of equality) impact models operation and points to an alignment challenge in getting models to follow instructions that might feel unnatural. 9.3.4 Equational Gap Filling This task is also subset of EQ1 with 200 proofs, but each proof has gap of randomly chosen size inserted at randomly chosen location. The model is asked to fill that gap with an appropriate sequence of steps. The output must be dictionary with two main keys, explanation and missingSteps. The value of explanation is plain text string describing the models reasoning strategy. The value of missingSteps is the main payload: the subproof filling the gap. This is expressed as list of dictionaries representing proof steps, each with two keys, term and supportingEquations. Thus, such list can be viewed as sequence of pairs (ti, Ni), 1 for some 1, where each ti is term (the value of the key term) and Ni is list of equation names (the value of the key supportingEquations). The specified steps correctly fill the gap iff the proof = t1 by N1 = t2 by N2 ... = tk by Nk = by ... (15) correctly derives the equation = , where is the term that appears on the step immediately before the gap and by is the step immediately after the gap. More precisely, we must have N1 t1; tk t; and ti1 Ni ti for every = 2, . . . , k, where is the symmetric closure of =S . (Thus, unlike ER, this task explicitly allows for bidirectional rewriting.) If the model determines that there is no way to fill the gap, it must output the empty list as the value of missingSteps. We write ?? to indicate the presence of gap. We explicitly require Ni 2, i.e., to avoid trivializing the problem, model may not cite more than 2 equations per step.
